title: 1492: Conquest of Paradise
studio: Cyrk / Légende Films / Due West / France 3 Cinéma / Gaumont / Scott Free Productions
lead_actors: Fernando Rey as Antonio de Marchena
Michael Wincott as Adrián de Moxica
Frank Langella as Luis de Santángel
awards: Golden Globe nomination: Best Original Score – Motion Picture (Vangelis, 50th Golden Globe Awards, 1993; lost to Alan Menken's Aladdin)
Echo Award (1996): International Artist of the Year – Vangelis
RTL Golden Lion Award (1996): Best Title Theme for a TV Film or Series – Vangelis
music: Score composed by Vangelis (Greek electronic composer)
Features the English Chamber Choir performing Latin hymns
Includes Flamenco guitarist-vocalists and indigenous-inspired instrumentation
Signature track: 'Conquest of Paradise' — became a major European hit in 1995
Album released October 9, 1992 on EastWest / Atlantic Records
maritime_connections: The entire film centers on Columbus's 1492 transatlantic voyages aboard three caravels (the Niña, Pinta, and Santa María). Maritime themes are pervasive: open-ocean navigation, celestial wayfinding, crew management and mutiny tensions, landfall in the Caribbean, and the logistical challenges of sustaining successive colonial expeditions across the Atlantic. The film offers extended sequences of blue-water sailing and depicts the psychological and physical toll of extended sea voyages on crews unfamiliar with ocean crossings of such duration.
Set entirely aboard two steamships crossing the Atlantic in 1899. The entire premise revolves around maritime mystery when the Kerberos encounters the ghost ship Prometheus adrift in the ocean.

title: 20,000 Leagues Under the Sea
maritime_connections: Based on Jules Verne's 1870 novel and incorporating elements from The Mysterious Island. The first feature-length adaptation follows Captain Nemo's submarine Nautilus on undersea adventures. Notable for using real underwater photography.

title: 20,000 Leagues Under the Sea
awards: Won 2 Academy Awards: Best Art Direction, Best Special Effects
maritime_connections: Adaptation of Jules Verne's novel, following the adventures of Captain Nemo aboard the submarine Nautilus.
reflection: Nemo has been compared to Ahab, but James Mason put Nemo's rage on the inside, making the character powerful but brittle at the same time. It was almost like he was the power source of the Nautilus, a boiler of barely contained power. I've always imagined Ahab as externalizing his rage, an exploding boiler. Ned Land repeatedly mutinies or tries to escape. Nemo, Bligh, Darth Vader, HAL 2000, The Joker, all the great villains seem to have an underlying vibration, like a bass note just a bit too low to hear, but that can be always felt and triggers the sixth sense of danger in the viewer even when danger is not imminent. Nemo almost always moved slowly, and spoke softly, like HAL 2000. Bligh (Laughton's Bligh) was different. He looked up under his eyebrows, like he didn't trust anything that anyone said. \

title: 72 Meters
maritime_connections: Russian submarine disaster drama about the crew of a submarine that becomes trapped on the seabed at 72 meters depth, inspired in part by the 2000 Kursk tragedy.

title: A High Wind in Jamaica
maritime_connections: Based on Richard Hughes' 1929 novel. A group of English children are accidentally taken captive by Caribbean pirates in the 19th century. Entirely set at sea aboard the pirates' brigantine, exploring an unsettling moral ambiguity.

title: A Sailor of the King
lead_actors: Jeffrey Hunter as Michael Doolan / Brown
Michael Rennie as Captain Richard Saville-Doolan
maritime_connections: Based on C.S. Forester's novel 'Brown on Resolution.' A young Royal Navy sailor is stranded on a Pacific island and single-handedly harasses a German cruiser, delaying it long enough for the British fleet to intercept. The captain of the cruiser is the sailor's own father — though neither knows the other is there.

title: A Twist of Sand
maritime_connections: Ex‑submarine commander smuggling diamonds off the Namibian coast.
reflection: Gritty smuggling story blending ex‑submariners and maritime adventure.

title: Above Us the Waves
lead_actors: John Gregson as Lieutenant Alec Duffy
Donald Sinden as Lieutenant Tom Corbett
James Robertson Justice as Admiral Ryder
maritime_connections: The story of midget 4-person British X-class submarines training and then attacking the *Tirpitz* in a Norwegian fjord in 1943.
reflection: This film confirmed what I already knew: I'm never, never getting into a midget submarine. I wasn't even comfortable 50 year ago getting into a fake *Nautilus* at the [20,000 Leagues Under the Sea Ride at Disney World](https://youtu.be/UFEVu5jcDqI?si=z3W8YhtZiDUDxQwo). *Above Us the Waves* (1955) tells the story of Operation Source, the somewhat successful British midget submarine attack on the German battleship *Tirpitz*. It was somewhat successful because it crippled the *Tirpitz* for six months, but all the subs were lost and their 4-person crews killed or captured. This movie's attack scenes were terrifying, with one sub trapped under the *Tirpitz* along with the explosives. But I question the ability of any wartime movie to properly capture the intensity of that kind of situation, as the studios like to make stone cold heroes out of the heroes who are just human. Maybe I should not blame the studios, as it is we viewers who like to see heroes as icons.

title: Action in the North Atlantic
lead_actors: Humphrey Bogart as Lieutenant Joe Rossi
Raymond Massey as Captain Steve Jarvis
awards: Nominated for Best Writing (Original Story) at the Academy Awards
maritime_connections: A World War II film that follows voyage of a liberty ship as they carry supplies from Halifax to Murmansk while facing attacks from German U-boats and planes.
reflection: This is a promotional movie trying to show that merchant seamen were just as much "men" as naval seamen, without disparaging naval seamen. The story itself was good, and the early scene with the sinking of the tanker a useful way of bonding the merchant crew who join together for the next trip like a band of brothers. I'm surprised the writing was nominated for an award, because it was full of tropes. The "walk inland until someone asks what an oar is" was old even then, I think. On the other hand, the movie had details that might be overlooked by contemporary set designers and directors, such as towing spars in fog to prevent collisions, and the Russian plane signalling "V" for victory by revving his engine in Morse code for "V." ([At 2:04](https://youtu.be/1mx4c0edHiM?si=SVRbTffx4_KthJqp))

title: Adventure Island
maritime_connections: Based on Robert Louis Stevenson and Lloyd Osbourne's novella Ebb Tide. A schooner captain and crew are stranded on a Pacific island ruled by a tyrannical despot. Also see: Ebb Tide (1922) and Ebb Tide (1937).

title: After the Storm
maritime_connections: Based on Ernest Hemingway's short story about a Florida Keys man who dives on a sunken luxury ocean liner after a hurricane to recover treasure, but finds himself unable to claim it.

title: Albert R.N.
lead_actors: Anthony Steel as Lt. Geoffrey Ainsworth
Jack Warner as Warrant Officer Griffiths
Robert Beatty as Lt. Cmdr. John Lawrence
maritime_connections: WWII film about Royal Navy officers in a German POW camp who construct a life-size dummy named Albert R.N. to fool guards during prisoner escapes. Not strictly maritime but follows Royal Navy officers.

title: All the Brothers Were Valiant
maritime_connections: Period maritime adventure based on Ben Ames Williams' novel. Two brothers command a whaling vessel; one returns transformed after discovering pearls, leading to conflict and a mutiny on the high seas.

title: Amistad
lead_actors: Matthew McConaughey as Roger Sherman Baldwin
Anthony Hopkins as John Quincy Adams
Nigel Hawthorne as Martin Van Buren
awards: Nominated for 4 Academy Awards: Best Supporting Actor (Anthony Hopkins), Best Cinematography, Best Costume Design, and Best Original Dramatic Score (John Williams)
Recognized for its historical significance and portrayal of the Amistad case
maritime_connections: This movie, which is about justice, has its beginnings aboard the slave ship *Amistad*, and flashes back to the ship.
reflection: This is really a justice film more than a maritime film. For me, it raises the question, why are there few, if any movies focused on describing and understanding the ways enslaved Africans rebelled against their slave-ship captors. There were so many slave ship mutinies and yet not one of them is a *Mutiny on the Bounty* (1935). That aside, *Amistad* (1997) is worth watching just to see Anthony Hopkins do his thing. Hopkins voices a robot in *Rebel Moon* (2023), and it is the best performance in the movie, among all the human characters.

title: Aquaman
lead_actors: Jason Momoa as Arthur Curry / Aquaman
Patrick Wilson as Orm / Ocean Master
Yahya Abdul-Mateen II as Black Manta
maritime_connections: DC superhero film set primarily underwater in the kingdom of Atlantis. Arthur Curry, half-human half-Atlantean, must claim the throne of Atlantis to prevent a war between the undersea world and the surface.

title: Around the World Under the Sea
lead_actors: Lloyd Bridges as Dr. Doug Standish
Brian Kelly as Dr. Craig Mosby
Shirley Eaton as Dr. Maggie Hanford
David McCallum as Dr. Phil Volker
maritime_connections: An international team of scientists travels the ocean floor in a nuclear submarine to plant earthquake-warning sensors around the world. Adventure and romance ensue during the global circumnavigation.

title: Assault on the Wayne
lead_actors: Leonard Nimoy as Commander Felix Morrow
Joseph Cotten as Admiral Yancy Niles
maritime_connections: A nuclear-armed submarine is gradually taken over from within by enemy agents among its crew. Leonard Nimoy plays the commander who must stop the conspiracy. Has a good twist ending.

title: Atlantic Convoy
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Atlantic%20Convoy%201942/awards/))
music: Studio library score; musical direction by Morris Stoloff (Columbia).
maritime_connections: WWII convoy escort operations.

title: Battle of the Coral Sea
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Battle%20of%20the%20Coral%20Sea%201959/awards/))
maritime_connections: WWII submarine action leading to the Battle of the Coral Sea.

title: Battleship
lead_actors: Taylor Kitsch as Lt. Alex Hopper
Rihanna as Petty Officer Cora Raikes
maritime_connections: Alien invasion film featuring U.S. Navy ships in a massive battle sequence.
reflection: A modern, effects‑heavy blockbuster with questionable plot but lots of naval spectacle.

title: Battleship Potemkin
lead_actors: Grigori Aleksandrov as Lieutenant S. Sergeyev
awards: In 1958, the film was voted on Brussels 12 list at the 1958 World Expo
Won the International Prize at the 1926 Venice Film Festival
Selected for preservation in the National Film Registry for its cultural and historical significance
Considered a pioneering work in the development of montage theory in cinema
music: Original score composed by Edmund Meisel (1925) for the German release, later re-scored for different versions
maritime_connections: This film is a dramatization of the mutiny aboard the real *Potemkin.* The real mutiny ended less well than the movie, but the mutineers survived in exile in Romania. The mutiny was considered a failure. However, it was a success in the film, as the fleet joined the mutiny at the end of the film.
reflection: The montage style and repeated scenes at the beginning were gripping. This is perhaps my favorite film in this log. The unlikely tangle of hammocks (at 0:02:23) and the worms on the meat leave no doubt as to status of the ratings aboard the *Potemkin*. While the staircase scene is considered the most notable, I liked the scene with the mutineers covered about to be executed. The reason is that this happened in the real mutiny, but almost surely the canvas was spread on the deck to prevent blood stains. Eisenstein must have appreciated the "collective" imagery of the sailors covered as a nameless group under a canvas cover.

title: Bear Island
maritime_connections: Based on Alistair MacLean's 1971 novel. A UN weather station expedition to the Norwegian island of Bjørnøya hides a deadly secret involving old U-boat pens and hidden Nazi gold. Nautical rather than strictly maritime — boats feature prominently.

title: Beneath the 12-Mile Reef
lead_actors: J. Carrol Naish as Thomas Rhys
maritime_connections: A Romeo and Juliet story among rival Greek and Conch sponge-diving families in the Florida Keys. One of the first CinemaScope films, featuring extensive underwater photography. A young Robert Wagner dives the treacherous 12-Mile Reef.

title: Billy Budd
lead_actors: Peter Ustinov as Captain Edwin Fairfax Vere
awards: Nominated for 1 Academy Award: Best Supporting Actor (Terence Stamp)
Terence Stamp won the Golden Globe for Most Promising Newcomer - Male
music: Score composed by Anthony Hopkins (not the actor)
maritime_connections: The story takes place at sea, aboard *Rights of Man* and *HMS Avenger*
reflection: As in the *Caine Mutiny*, the wrong man was on trial. There must be something we as Western film watchers like about injustice, followed by some kind of redemption. In the *Caine Mutiny*, Keefer gets put down in front of his peers at a party. In the movie *Billy Budd*, just after the hanging, the crew established control and then fight the French, just as in *Damn the Defiant* (1962). But Budd was both guilty and innocent at the same time and in the story, redemption is not the crew fighting the French, rather the canonization of Budd. Ustinov was an interesting Royal Navy captain. Often, they are one-dimensional (not Aubry, not Hornblower) like Bligh or Queeg, with little concern for nuanced interpretation of sea law.

title: Black Sea
maritime_connections: An incompatible mix of Russian and English salvagers hire an old Soviet submarine to recover a cache of Nazi gold from a sunken U-boat on the Black Sea floor. Greed and cultural tensions between the crews turn deadly.

title: Blood Vessel
awards: Won Best Art Department, Best Costume, Best Supporting Actress at FilmQuest 2019
maritime_connections: Survivors of a torpedoed hospital ship board a drifting German minesweeper during WWII, only to discover it's haunted by vampires.
reflection: Blending horror with WWII naval settings, *Blood Vessel* innovates like *Overlord* (2018) by infusing supernatural elements into maritime warfare. It expands the genre beyond historical accuracy, exploring how ghost ships symbolize lingering war traumas, akin to *Below* (2002) in submarine horror.

title: Britannic
maritime_connections: Fictionalized retelling of the sinking of *HMHS Britannic*, sister ship to *Titanic*. Involves espionage and sabotage aboard the vessel.
reflection: Leans into spy-thriller territory but makes excellent use of maritime set pieces. Less melodramatic than *Titanic* (1997), but still visually gripping.

title: Captain Horatio Hornblower
lead_actors: Gregory Peck as Captain Horatio Hornblower
Virginia Mayo as Lady Barbara Wellesley
Robert Beatty as Lt. William Bush
Terence Morgan as First Lt. Gerard
James Robertson Justice as Seaman Quist
maritime_connections: C. S. Forester's naval hero
reflection: This may be the worst film in my log, even worse than *2010: Moby Dick* (2010). It is really two movies cobbled together. The first part takes place in the Pacific Ocean, and when that is done the second part takes place in the Mediterranean Sea. The sets are worse than an earlier film *Down to the Sea in Ships* (1922) and a later film, *Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World* (27). Gregory Peck fails to capture the subtle insecurities of Hornblower, and the director failed to capture the humorous relationship between Hornblower and his steward. Plus, it seems that nearly every helm order was carried out incorrectly.

title: Captain Kidd
lead_actors: Charles Laughton as Captain William Kidd
Barbara Britton as Lady Anne Dunstan
maritime_connections: Historical adventure based loosely on the life of the real privateer-turned-pirate Captain William Kidd. Features period tall ship scenes as Kidd sails under commission from the British Crown while pursuing his own treacherous agenda.

title: Captain Nemo and the Underwater City
lead_actors: Chuck Connors as Senator Robert Fraser
maritime_connections: Jules Verne-inspired sequel in which survivors of a shipwreck are rescued by Captain Nemo and brought to his domed underwater city. They must choose between staying in the paradise beneath the sea or trying to return to the surface.

title: Captain Phillips
awards: BAFTA Award for Best Supporting Actor ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Captain%20Phillips%202013/awards/))
maritime_connections: True story of 2009 Maersk Alabama hijacking.

title: Captain Ron
lead_actors: Mary Kay Place as Katherine Harvey
awards: Young Artist Award nominations for Meadow Sisto and Benjamin Salisbury
maritime_connections: The story follows a family who inherits a dilapidated yacht in the Caribbean and hires the eccentric Captain Ron to sail it to Miami, leading to a series of comedic misadventures at sea involving pirates, guerrilla encounters, and the challenges of amateur sailing in tropical waters.
reflection: _Captain Ron_ (1992) is similar to the 1991 film _What about Bob?_ (1992). In both movies, an eccentric outsider disrupts a family's vacation. But Bob (Bill Murry) is a much more sympathetic character than Kurt Russell's Ron. _What about Bob?_ (1992) is endearing; _Captain Ron_ (1992) is just long, and the scenes about the sailboat, especially in the engine room, are implausible to the point of silly. At one point, after Captain Ron has been dismissed as captain, the Harvey family are captured by Cuban pirates. Ron appears without explaination to save them. The movie doesn't make sense. The thing to do is watch _Captain Ron_ (1992) and _What about Bob?_ (1992) back to back and compare them, and ask, why is one better?

title: Captain Roughneck from St. Pauli
lead_actors: Curd Jürgens as Capt. Markus Jolly
Johanna von Koczian as Anne Marie Jansen
maritime_connections: A Hamburg ship captain transports a vaccine to a Latin American port but faces theft and false accusations, leading to an adventurous pursuit.
reflection: This German adventure evokes the swashbuckling spirit of *Captain Blood* (1935) but in a modern cargo context, highlighting captains as rogue heroes. In maritime films' global tapestry, it adds European flair to seafaring tales, similar to *The Captain's Table* (1959) in mixing comedy with shipboard intrigue.

title: Close Quarters
lead_actors: Real submarine crew (documentary-style, no lead actors)
maritime_connections: An authentic depiction of a routine Royal Navy submarine patrol in the North Sea off the coast of Norway during World War II, showcasing the cramped conditions and dangers faced by the crew.
reflection: Close Quarters* exemplifies the documentary impulse in wartime maritime cinema, similar to how *Western Approaches* (1944) blended real footage with dramatization to honor merchant seamen. By using actual submariners rather than actors, it provides an unvarnished look at the claustrophobic reality of undersea warfare, underscoring how such films served not just as propaganda but as vital records of naval life, paving the way for more immersive submarine stories like *Das Boot* (1981) that explore the psychological depths of isolation at sea.

title: Corvette K-225
lead_actors: Randolph Scott as Lt. Cmdr. MacClain
James Brown as Lt. Paul Cartwright
awards: Nominated for Academy Award for Best Cinematography (Black-and-White)
maritime_connections: The film chronicles a Canadian corvette's perilous convoy escort duty across the Atlantic during World War II.
reflection: Corvette K-225* pays tribute to Allied escort vessels, much like *San Demetrio London* (1943) honors tanker crews. It captures the convoy system's vital role in maritime history, influencing films like *Greyhound* (2020) that dramatize the Battle of the Atlantic's relentless U-boat threats.

title: Counter Measures
maritime_connections: Low-budget direct-to-video submarine action thriller in which a rogue officer seizes control of a nuclear submarine.

title: Crash Dive
awards: Academy Award for Best Special Effects ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Crash%20Dive%201943/awards/))
maritime_connections: WWII submarine warfare.

title: Crash Dive
maritime_connections: Low-budget action thriller in which a Navy SEAL must retake a nuclear submarine seized by terrorists. Note: unrelated to the 1943 film of the same name.

title: Crimson Tide
studio: Hollywood Pictures / Don Simpson-Jerry Bruckheimer Films
lead_actors: Denzel Washington as Lt. Commander Ron Hunter
Gene Hackman as Captain Frank Ramsey
George Dzundza as Chief of the Boat (COB) Walters
Viggo Mortensen as Lt. Peter Ince
James Gandolfini as Lt. Bobby Dougherty
Matt Craven as Lt. Roy Zimmer
awards: Won Academy Award for Best Sound Editing
Nominated for Best Film Editing at the Academy Awards
Won a Grammy Award for Best Instrumental Composition Written for a Motion Picture (Hans Zimmer)
Recognized as one of the most intense and well-crafted submarine thrillers of all time
maritime_connections: Most of the movie takes place underwater aboard the fictitious USS *Alabama.*
reflection: Crimson Tide* is a thriller about a battle of wills between a sub captain and his XO who have conflicting leadership styles and philosophies of war ([as in this scene](https://youtu.be/hur6LcyuTuU?si=VKvaohdstEs3v9V2)). Gene Hackman's Captain Ramsey represents old-school military authority, while Denzel Washington's Lt. Commander Hunter embodies a more global analytical, measured approach to war. This film is like *Billy Budd* (1962) in that Hunter is both right and wrong at the same time. He mutinies but is also clearly justified in his mutiny. (Of course, Budd did not mutiny.) This film is different from other mutiny films in that the captain takes back his ship by force, something uncommon in mutiny movies. In some movies (e.g., *Voyage of the Dammed* (1776) and *Damn the Defiant* (1962)) the mutineers give up the mutiny for a higher cause or after a convincing argument by the captain. Also different in this movie is that Captain Ramsey is generally well liked. The mutiny's underpinning is not a dispute over conditions or treatment of crew, rather the decision to launch nuclear weapons. Thus, it is more like *The Caine Mutiny* (1954) had Queeg been a little more likeable. However, Queeg was inept and wrong, Ramsey was competent and wrong. So, it seems like being wrong is grounds for mutiny. However, in both films, the abrasiveness of the captains sets the undertone for the mutiny.

title: Dangerous Crossing
lead_actors: Michael Rennie as Dr. Paul Manning
maritime_connections: Mystery thriller set aboard a transatlantic ocean liner. A newlywed woman's husband mysteriously disappears immediately after boarding, and no one on the ship has any record of him ever existing. Also known as 'Vanishing Voyage.' See also the separate entry under that title.

title: Death Ship
lead_actors: Sally Ann Howes as Margaret Marshall
maritime_connections: Survivors of a cruise ship collision board a mysterious freighter that turns out to be a haunted WWII Nazi torture vessel.
reflection: Death Ship* transforms the ghost ship trope from *The Ghost Ship* (1943) into outright horror, using maritime isolation for supernatural dread. It exemplifies 1980s slasher influences on sea tales, paralleling *Ghost Ship* (2002) in exploiting abandoned vessels as metaphors for unresolved wartime atrocities.

title: Deep Waters
maritime_connections: Maine lobster-fishing drama in which a free-spirited fisherman takes a troubled orphan boy under his wing. Set entirely in the world of Maine commercial fishing with authentic coastal scenery and seamanship.

title: Depth Charge
lead_actors: Eric Balfour as Lt. Cmdr. Jack Dyson
maritime_connections: TV thriller in which a rogue admiral seizes control of a US nuclear submarine and threatens to launch its missiles. A young officer must stop him before it is too late.

title: Destroyer
lead_actors: Edward G. Robinson as Steve Boleslavski
awards: Nominated for three Academy Awards: Best Art Direction, Best Assistant Director, Best Scoring
maritime_connections: An aging shipbuilder reenlists in the Navy to serve on a destroyer he helped build, facing trials during WWII Pacific operations.
reflection: Destroyer* celebrates shipyard workers' contributions, similar to *Action in the North Atlantic* (1943) in honoring merchant marine efforts. It reflects wartime propaganda's emphasis on homefront-to-frontline transitions, influencing films like *The Fighting Seabees* (1944) that valorize construction in naval contexts.

title: Devil and the Deep
maritime_connections: A submarine commander goes mad with jealousy over his wife's affairs and deliberately lets his submarine be rammed, a kind of murder-suicide at sea. Laughton is memorably unhinged in one of his first major American film roles.

title: Down to the Sea in Ships
lead_actors: Raymond McKee as Thomas Allan Dexter
William Walcott as Charles W. Morgan
music: Originally a silent film with live musical accompaniment; later versions feature added scores
maritime_connections: Great scenes of actual whaling plus a mutiny
reflection: This silent-era maritime film has a great plot and subplots. It is interesting to compare the production quality of this film with *Captain Hornblower* (1951) and *Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World* (2003), because there was not a steady improvement over time in production quality. *Down to the Sea in Ships* (1922; there were movies with the same title in 1936 and 1949) and *Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World* (2003) are both much more realistic that *Captain Hornblower* (1951). Realism isn't absolutely necessary to make a film work, but it helps. As a kid I was influence by "The Rope's End" by Reginald Hegarty, which is a 1914 travelogue of Hegarty's voyage with his family as a boy (about 8 years old) on the whaling bark *Alice Knowles* out of New Bedford. His father was the captain. It was great to put together Heagerty's descriptions of whaling with actual film.

title: Down to the Sea in Ships
lead_actors: Lionel Barrymore as Captain Bering Joy
maritime_connections: Set in the world of New England sperm-whale hunting. An old whaling captain hopes his grandson will carry on the tradition, but must first deal with a treacherous first mate. Uses authentic whaling vessel footage.

title: Dunkirk
lead_actors: John Mills as Corporal Tubby Binns
maritime_connections: British WWII drama depicting the 1940 evacuation of Dunkirk from multiple perspectives — stranded soldiers, a civilian skipper, and a journalist. Features the armada of small boats crossing the Channel under fire.

title: Ebb Tide
maritime_connections: Silent film adaptation of Robert Louis Stevenson and Lloyd Osbourne's novella about three desperate men who take over a plague ship drifting in the South Pacific. See also: Ebb Tide (1937) and Adventure Island (1947).

title: Ebb Tide
lead_actors: Oscar Homolka as Capt. Jakob Thorbecke
music: Theme song "Ebb Tide" composed by Leo Robin and Ralph Rainger
maritime_connections: Based on a Robert Louis Stevenson novel, the story involves expatriates sailing a schooner in the South Seas, delivering cargo amid storms and intrigue.
reflection: Adapting Stevenson's adventure, *Ebb Tide* evokes the romantic escapism of South Seas tales like *Mutiny on the Bounty* (1935), using the ocean as a backdrop for moral decay and redemption. It represents pre-war maritime films' blend of exoticism and peril, setting the stage for post-war realism in sea adventures.

title: Fire Down Below
maritime_connections: A love-hate triangle set entirely in the maritime world of the Caribbean. Mitchum and Lemmon are small-time smugglers who transport Hayworth — a woman with a mysterious past and no papers — aboard their boat into Cuba.

title: Fury at Smugglers Bay
maritime_connections: Cornwall-set period adventure about a smuggling gang terrorising the coast. A local squire appears to be in league with the smugglers while his son investigates the truth. Features coastal cliff scenery and small-boat action.

title: Ghosts of Amistad
lead_actors: Marcus Rediker (Historian, Narrator, and Project Leader)
Various villagers and historians in Sierra Leone
music: Traditional African music and oral storytelling featured throughout the documentary
maritime_connections: Scholars travel to the home places of returned *Amistad* survivors in search of oral histories.
reflection: The Ghosts of Amistad* (2014) is an attempt for a human look at history, and that the ghosts of the Amistad are living memories that still shape the present. But it didn't really work out that way. I sympathize with Rediker---he had to make something from nothing, a common occurrence in the sciences when a funded project produces no good results, but a paper must still be published no matter what. *Ghosts of Amistad* was a good idea, to visit the homeland of the *Amistad* slaves, which some returned to, and ask the current residents about their memories of the event. However, almost no one had any an oral record of the event. Also, the movie had a very tourist-home-movie feel, much different from Cousteau's *The Silent World* (1956), which had motion and action. Much of Rediker's film was simply talking heads. I don't think there is an excuse for that, because Ken Burn's *Civil War* was much the same idea, with lots of interviews, but Burns managed to find tension in his material.

title: Gods of the Deep
maritime_connections: A deep-sea crew investigating a sunken facility awakens a Lovecraftian sea god.
reflection: A pulpy homage to ’70s and ’80s aquatic horror. Campy, murky, and oddly committed to its cosmic themes. It’s like *The Beyond* with SCUBA tanks.

title: Going Under
lead_actors: Wendy Schaal as Lt. Holly Pierce
Ned Beatty as Captain Fred T. Slade
maritime_connections: Comedy about the hapless crew of an experimental US Navy submarine. A bumbling new officer tries to prove himself while the submarine lurches from one disaster to another.

title: Gorgo
music: Score composed by Angelo Francesco Lavagnino
maritime_connections: A salvage ship crew captures a giant prehistoric sea creature off the Irish coast and brings it to London as a circus attraction — not realising it is only a juvenile. Its enormous mother then rises from the sea to retrieve her offspring.

title: Gray Lady Down
lead_actors: Charlton Heston as Captain Paul Blanchard
maritime_connections: A US nuclear submarine is rammed by a Norwegian freighter in fog and sinks to a ledge on the continental shelf. A rescue operation using a small experimental deep-sea vehicle races to save the trapped crew before their oxygen runs out.

title: Harbinger Down
awards: No major awards; cult following for practical effects
maritime_connections: Set aboard a crabbing vessel in the Bering Sea where the crew recovers a Soviet spacecraft containing alien life.
reflection: Essentially *The Thing* at sea, with throwback practical monster effects. Its isolated maritime setting amplifies the horror. Cold, claustrophobic, and gooey.

title: Hell and High Water
lead_actors: Richard Widmark as Capt. Adam Jones
Bella Darvi as Professor Denise Gerard
awards: Academy Award nomination for Best Special Effects
maritime_connections: Cold War submarine thriller — civilians repurpose a Japanese WWII submarine to stop a potential atomic war.
reflection: A sub movie with civilians aboard a repurposed Japanese WWII sub, trying to stop an atomic war. Incredibly misogynistic and racist.

title: Hell Below Zero
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Hell%20Below%20Zero%201954/awards/))
maritime_connections: Adventure in Antarctic whaling waters.
reflection: Whaling‑based maritime thriller with Alan Ladd. Excellent scenes of 20th century whaling.

title: Hell Boats
lead_actors: James Franciscus as Lt. Cmdr. Jeffords
maritime_connections: The film follows a British Motor Torpedo Boat flotilla on a perilous mission to destroy a German shore base in the Mediterranean during World War II.
reflection: Hell Boats* captures the high-speed peril of coastal forces operations, echoing the daring raids in *The Cockleshell Heroes* (1955) while highlighting the often-underrated role of small craft in disrupting enemy supply lines. Though laced with a contrived love triangle, it reflects the era's shift toward more personal war stories, reminding us how maritime films evolved from broad epics to intimate tales of sacrifice, much like *The Enemy Below* (1957) personalized submarine duels.

title: Hellcats of the Navy
lead_actors: Ronald Reagan as Cmdr. Casey Abbott
Nancy Davis (Reagan) as Nurse Lt. Helen Blair
Arthur Franz as Lt. Cmdr. Don Landon
Robert Arthur as Lt. Paul Prentice
Harry Lauter as Lt. (j.g.) Wes Barton
Fleet Adm. Chester W. Nimitz as himself (uncredited)
music: Score conducted by Mischa Bakaleinikoff (Columbia Pictures music department)
maritime_connections: One of the most submarine-centric films in Hollywood history. The entire plot unfolds aboard the USS Starfish, a U.S. Navy submarine operating in the Sea of Japan during the final stages of WWII. The mission — charting enemy minefields in the Tsushima Strait and severing Japanese supply lines — is drawn from actual Navy operations documented in the nonfiction source book 'Hellcats of the Sea' by Vice Admiral Charles A. Lockwood and Col. Hans Christian Adamson. The film features extended sequences of submarine maneuvering, frogman operations, mine-avoidance, and the command ethics of undersea warfare. Real Admiral Chester W. Nimitz appears on camera in an introductory segment lending the film an unusual documentary authority.
reflection: Compare Reagan's performance to Clark Cable's _Run Silent, Run Deep_ performance. A few notes: this was the only joint film appearance by Ronald Reagan and Nancy Davis, who had been married since 1952. The film has no notable awards record — it was a B-movie production with a drab supporting cast — but carries lasting historical curiosity as the only feature pairing a future U.S. President and First Lady. The score is credited to Mischa Bakaleinikoff, the longtime head of Columbia's music department, rather than an independently commissioned composer, which was standard practice for modest studio productions of the era.

title: Heweliusz
maritime_connections: Polish docudrama about the sinking of the Polish ferry MS Heweliusz in the Baltic Sea in January 1993, in which 55 of the 68 people aboard perished in a storm.

title: High Seas
maritime_connections: Spanish mystery series (Alta Mar) set aboard a 1940s luxury ocean liner sailing from Spain to Brazil. When murders begin to occur, two sisters investigate the dark secrets hiding among the passengers and crew. Three seasons.

title: Hostile Waters
lead_actors: Max von Sydow as Admiral Chernavin
maritime_connections: Dramatisation of the 1986 collision between the Soviet nuclear submarine K-219 and a US submarine in the Atlantic, and the subsequent onboard fire and near-nuclear disaster. Based on the book by Tom Clancy and Martin Greenberg.

title: Houseboat
maritime_connections: Romantic comedy set largely aboard a dilapidated houseboat on the Potomac where a widower and his three children set up home, joined by an Italian heiress masquerading as a domestic. The houseboat setting is central to the film's charm.

title: Humanoids from the Deep
lead_actors: Ann Turkel as Dr. Susan Drake
maritime_connections: Mutated sea creatures, spawned by scientific tampering, terrorize a coastal fishing town, killing residents and assaulting women.
reflection: Exploiting eco-horror like *The Horror of Party Beach* (1964), *Humanoids from the Deep* warns of oceanic pollution's consequences while delivering B-movie thrills. It marks a schlocky turn in maritime monster films, influencing creature features like *Deep Rising* (1998) that blend gore with sea-based chaos.

title: Hunter Killer
lead_actors: Gerard Butler as Capt. Joe Glass
Gary Oldman as Chairman of the Joint Chiefs
Common as Rear Admiral John Fisk
maritime_connections: A US submarine captain rescues a Russian submarine commander and uncovers a plot by a rogue Russian general. The mission evolves into a covert operation deep inside Russian waters to prevent a global war.

title: In Pursuit of the Graf Spee
maritime_connections: Dramatisation of the 1939 Battle of the River Plate — the first naval battle of WWII. Three British cruisers engage the German pocket battleship Admiral Graf Spee, driving her into Montevideo harbour where she is ultimately scuttled. Also known as 'The Battle of the River Plate.'

title: In the Heart of the Sea
awards: Nominated for Best Special Visual Effects (BAFTA)
maritime_connections: Based on the 1820 tragedy of the whaleship *Essex*, which inspired *Moby-Dick*. Extensive whaling scenes, storms, and shipwreck survival.
reflection: A grim reminder of the brutality of whaling and the ocean’s indifference. The movie doesn’t flinch from starvation, desperation, or cannibalism. A darker take on seafaring lore.

title: In Which We Serve
lead_actors: Noël Coward as Captain E.V. Kinross
John Mills as Ordinary Seaman Shorty Blake
Bernard Miles as Chief Petty Officer Hardy
awards: Nominated for 2 Academy Awards: Best Picture and Best Original Screenplay (Noël Coward)
Won the New York Film Critics Circle Award for Best Director (Noël Coward & David Lean)
maritime_connections: The film is a combination of at-sea shots and flashbacks to the home life of the captain (Noel Coward) and several sailors.
reflection: Unlike action-driven war films, *In Which We Serve* focuses on the bonds between sailors and their families, emphasizing duty and honor in the face of war's brutality. The movie could have been titled, *This Is Why We Serve*. For an actor of Coward's status, I was surprised at his stiff acting style and the somewhat monotone delivery. Coward's own role in the war was to keep up moral, and he felt that family was key, that servicemen were fighting for their homes, no matter their social status.

title: Into the Deep
maritime_connections: A thriller set on a disabled sailing yacht where a group of friends encounters deceit and violence amid isolation at sea.
reflection: Into the Deep* modernizes the isolated peril of open-water thrillers like *Dead Calm* (1989), using the confines of a yacht to amplify psychological tension. Within maritime cinema's evolution, it shifts focus from grand naval battles to intimate horrors, reflecting how contemporary films explore personal vulnerabilities at sea, much like *Adrift* (2018) blends survival with emotional depth.

title: It Came from Beneath the Sea
lead_actors: Kenneth Tobey as Cmdr. Pete Matthews
Faith Domergue as Prof. Lesley Joyce
Donald Curtis as Dr. John Carter
maritime_connections: Sci-fi thriller in which H-bomb testing in the Pacific disturbs a colossal radioactive octopus that attacks shipping and eventually assaults San Francisco. Features Ray Harryhausen's stop-motion special effects and US Navy submarine sequences.

title: Jaws
lead_actors: Roy Scheider as Chief Martin Brody
awards: Won 3 Academy Awards: Best Film Editing, Best Original Dramatic Score (John Williams), and Best Sound
Nominated for Best Picture at the Academy Awards
Recognized by the American Film Institute as one of the greatest thrillers of all time
Inducted into the National Film Registry for its cultural, historical, and aesthetic significance
maritime_connections: A shark is a central character.
reflection: Between the clashes with the Shark, there are conflicts among Hooper, Quint, the Mayor and city selectmen, and Brody. But those are all bread-and-butter conflicts typical for movies. Spielberg (and Scheider) makes Brody complex, by not bragging about his scar aboard the *Orca*. At one point, aboard the *Orca*, Brody has a brief, unsuccessful mutiny put down but Quint. That mutiny was based on an external fear of the Shark, as was Johann's in *Das Boot* (1981) of depth charges. Spielberg makes the Shark something we are interested in, and to make the point simply view *Jaws II*, which a movie with nothing but jump scares. In that movie, the shark really is just a mechanical device. I'm not alone in having the "Indianapolis Speech," and scar comparison (especially Brody's silence about his scar) as a [favorite clip](https://youtu.be/u9S41Kplsbs?si=hErzmjguJ-gmqODf).

title: Krakatoa, East of Java
maritime_connections: In 1883, a ship captain leads a salvage mission in the Dutch East Indies to recover pearls from a sunken wreck, but the expedition is overtaken by the catastrophic eruption of Krakatoa. Uses a real tall ship refurbished for the film. Note: the title is geographically incorrect — Krakatoa is west of Java.

title: Kursk
lead_actors: Colin Firth as Commodore David Russell
Max von Sydow as Admiral Grudzinsky
maritime_connections: Dramatisation of the August 2000 sinking of the Russian nuclear submarine Kursk in the Barents Sea, in which 118 sailors perished. The film follows both the trapped crew and the political and bureaucratic failures that prevented a timely rescue. Also released as 'Kursk: The Last Mission' (UK) and 'The Command' (US).

title: Last Breath
studio: MetFilm, Floating Harbour, Crab Apple Films, Backflip Media
maritime_connections: The story takes place underwater in the North Sea. It involves commercial saturation diving; the diver is working beneath the ocean, tethered by an umbilical to a support vessel, which drifts and severs the connection.
reflection: Not yet screened.

title: Last Breath
studio: Longshot Films; Dark Castle Entertainment; Gold Circle Films; MetFilm
lead_actors: Cliff Curtis as Captain André Jenson
awards: Winner — *Truly Moving Picture Award* for director Alex Parkinson
maritime_connections: Based on a true incident involving saturation divers in the North Sea. The diving bell’s umbilical is severed after a ship malfunction, stranding one diver underwater with minimal oxygen. The story follows the rescue attempt both underwater and from the surface support crew.
reflection: It seemed very real to me, although I did not get the sense of isolation that surely must be a part of saturation diving. These divers are, for all intents and purposes, four days from the nearest help for anything. That's becuause four days is how long it takes to decompress. So, a diver with appendicitis could be physically five feet from a surgeon but not be able to get help until decompression.

title: Last Resort
lead_actors: André Braugher as Capt. Marcus Chaplin
Scott Speedman as XO Sam Kendal
Daisy Betts as Lt. Grace Shepard
maritime_connections: US nuclear submarine USS Colorado picks up Navy SEALs and receives an order to launch nuclear missiles. When the captain refuses to fire without verification, he is relieved of command — and the crew takes refuge on a remote island to survive. 13 episodes. Creators: Shawn Ryan and Karl Gajdusek.

title: Leviathan
awards: Nominated for Saturn Award (Best Horror Film)
maritime_connections: Underwater mining crew encounters a parasitic mutation on a sunken Soviet ship.
reflection: Clearly riding the coattails of *The Abyss* and *Alien*, but it has its charms. Confined submarine spaces make the horror plausible. The corporate greed subplot still resonates.

title: Lifeboat
lead_actors: Henry Hull as Charles D. Rittenhouse
awards: Nominated for 3 Academy Awards: Best Director (Alfred Hitchcock), Best Story (John Steinbeck), and Best Cinematography (Glen MacWilliams)
Recognized as one of Hitchcock's most unique and experimental films
Selected for preservation in the National Film Registry for its cultural, historical, and aesthetic significance
maritime_connections: The entire movie takes place in a lifeboat at sea after a ship is torpedoed by a U-boat, which is also sunk. The survivors on the lifeboat are passengers and crew. The members of the lifeboat also rescue the U-boat's captain, and later in the movie, another German sailor.
reflection: This film stands out for, among other things, the rampant distrust among survivors. In every film screened so far this semester, only this one starts with an almost completely dysfunctional groups of people who, for the most part, do not know each other well if at all. They are not only dysfunctional as sailors (only Willy, the U-boat captain, is knowledgeable) but they their personalities inhibit survival---that's how Willy is able to essential take control of the survivors and the lifeboat. By the end of the movie the survivors pull together as a team.

title: Long John Silver’s Return to Treasure Island
Lord Jim
awards: Nominated for Golden Globe (Best Original Score)
maritime_connections: Adapted from Joseph Conrad’s novel about a disgraced ship officer seeking redemption in Southeast Asia.
reflection: O’Toole brings tragic gravitas to the title role. The ship desertion scene is unforgettable. A film that grapples with guilt and the ethics of command.

title: Lusitania: Murder on the Atlantic (TV Movie)
Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World
studio: 20th Century Fox / Universal Pictures / Miramax Films
lead_actors: Russell Crowe as Captain Jack Aubrey
Paul Bettany as Dr. Stephen Maturin
James D'Arcy as First Lieutenant Tom Pullings
awards: Won 2 Academy Awards: Best Cinematography (Russell Boyd) and Best Sound Editing
Nominated for 10 Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Director
music: Score composed by Iva Davies, Christopher Gordon, and Richard Tognetti
Features classical pieces from Mozart, Bach, and Boccherini
maritime_connections: British Navy during the Napoleonic era.
reflection: The best part of Patrick O'Brian's books and the movie is Captain Jack Aubrey's relationship with Dr. Maturin. While conflict, as in *Jaws* (1975), seems to be the cornerstone of tension that drives plots, I like plots with characters that generally get along. A good example would be the original *Star Trek* (1966) series or even *The Cruel Sea* (1953) in which Lt. Bennett and Captain Ericson are a team throughout the movie. The seagoing cinematography in this film is particularly strong, and it is hard to distinguish between shots filmed on the real ship *Rose*, the models, the stages, and CGI. For comparison, watch *The Sea Wolf* (1951) and *Moby Dick* (2011), the former being among the worst and the latter the best showing real ships sailed on the real ocean. Here is a video on the [*filming of *Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World (2003)](https://youtu.be/_yLl05D0evQ?si=2b9BZM0Ev2MS-4Vj) where the production crew explains the challenges of filming at sea, the building of the miniatures (which were huge) and the cast's training program.

title: Men Without Women
maritime_connections: US Navy divers race to save the crew of a submarine foundered on the ocean floor while the trapped sailors prepare to die. The full sound version is lost; only the silent 'International Sound Version' held by the Museum of Modern Art survives.

title: Middle Passage
lead_actors: Djimon Hounsou as the Narrator (US version; voiceover for enslaved African)
A cast of mostly non-speaking actors representing enslaved Africans and crew
awards: Screened at Cannes Film Festival (Un Certain Regard, 2000)
maritime_connections: A description of a Middle Passage Voyage
reflection: Most Middle Passage literature (there aren't many movies) comes from accounts by European traders or abolitionist narratives, or databases like SlaveVoyages.org, but *Middle Passage* (2000) centers on the African experience, reclaiming historical agency through art. The narrative was written by Walter Mosley. This is an experimental historical film, recreating a Middle Passage Voyage with a narrative that could be called a prose poem, and cinematography that could also be called a prose poem. The movie is meditative and haunting. In is biography, director Guy Deslauriers said that slavery was not mentioned during his childhood on Guadalupe and this film is an attempt to account recognize the horrors of the Middle Passage. Rather than using a traditional plot structure, director Guy Deslauriers immerses the viewer in a dreamlike, sensory experience, combining voiceover narration with slightly disorienting close-up shots, disorienting in the sense that the viewer is not given a location from which to view the events. Its slow pace combined with relentless imagery make it feel like waves upon waves of suffering and survival coming from the screen. The film is hard to find on a streaming service, but this is the [trailer](https://youtu.be/3nRgboJR1hs?si=QmDxGF7UB4Qi0AOk).

title: Mission of the Shark: The Saga of the USS Indianapolis (TV Movie)
Moby Dick
maritime_connections: An origin story for Captain Ahab: the film opens with Ahab as a cocky young man with two legs before his encounter with the White Whale. Ends very differently from Melville — Ahab kills Moby Dick and gets the girl. Contains brief footage of actual whaling operations.

title: Moby Dick
awards: None (But Orson Welles deserves something for his Father Mapple.)
maritime_connections: The story mostly takes place aboard the Whaler *Pequod.*
reflection: Screenplay by Ray Bradbury and directed by John Huston and it shows. The script and movie flow better than the other *Moby Dick*s. In other words, the craftsmanship of the storytelling seems well tuned and better than the other films. Let's get it out in the open now: For Ahabs, prefer Peck to Stewart, and Stewart to Hurt. I actually think Stewart was closer to the "real" Ahab, who has moments that seem lucid and moments that seem crazy in the Shakespearean way that Stewart excels at performing. But Peck's Ahab was good for the screen---a simpler character that was easy to understand in movie, more passion and less instability, he's brooding and smouldering, whereas Steward has flashes of lighting. Hurt's performance understated to the point where I would not follow him after the white whale if he asked me to. I did like the way he tied himself to Moby Dick at the end of the film, though. \

title: Moby Dick
release_date: March 15, 1998 (USA, TV miniseries on USA Network)
studio: Hallmark Entertainment / Francis Ford Coppola's American Zoetrope
awards: Nominated for several Emmy Awards, including Outstanding Miniseries
Gregory Peck won a Golden Globe for Best Supporting Actor in a Series, Miniseries, or Motion Picture Made for Television
maritime_connections: The story mostly takes place aboard the Whaler *Pequod.*
reflection: A made-for-TV miniseries, *Moby Dick (1998)* is more than a retelling of Melville's novel, it serves as both homage and reinterpretation. Patrick Stewart's Ahab is fierce, intelligent, and driven---he doesn't really brood. Ted Levine (the killer in *The Silence of the Lambs*, 1991) who is a very sound actor plays Starbuck, and, as in the novel, offers a moral---and business---voice amid the madness. However, I imagined Starbuck more like Leo Geen played him, an accountant type, rather than coming across a bit lost and weak, as Levine played the role. Maybe the role should have been played by another actor with Shakespearian experience. [Ralph Fiennes might do](https://youtu.be/v6ji07tsI2M?si=NOc-TJnyA5tDXcJr). Starbuck's role in the movies is less than Ahab's, Ishamel's and Queequeg's, but maybe that is a mistake. Perhaps a larger role for Starbuck would counterpoint Ahab and give Ahab greater dimensionality.

title: Moby Dick
release_date: November 23, 2010 (USA, television release via The Asylum/Syfy)
lead_actors: Renée O'Connor as Dr. Michelle Herman
maritime_connections: The *Pequod* is a US nuclear submarine
reflection: Douglas Hoefstader, who won the Pulitzer Prize in 1979 for *Gödel, Escher, Bach: An Eternal Golden Braid* also wrote a book about the challenges and meanings of translating literature from one language to another, called *Le Ton beau de Marot: In Praise of the Music of Language* (1997). In that book, for me, he convincingly blurred the line between "exact translation" and translating the meaning of something. For him the best translation of the poem, "Le Ton beau de Marot" was one by his mother, who is not a French reader, but captured the vibe of the poem. That's what I think about the B-movie adaptation of [*Moby Dick (2010)*](https://youtu.be/lXSHCzCuKzg?si=EZt_4cI9Ct04k_nt) where Barry Bostwick plays Ahab as a one-legged nuclear submarine captain obsessive in pursuit of a 500-foot prehistoric monster. Stubb is there, as is Flask, Pip, and others. Ishmael is now Michelle, a whale expert. The film replaces philosophical depth with explosive set pieces, poor prop construction, and the worst CGI, but without excuses. Unlike other versions that stay close to the novel's themes of fate and madness, this *Moby Dick* embraces camp, making it less a meditation on man versus nature and more a mash-up of Melville and *Jaws*. It's not high art, but it's high-energy and highly entertaining so long as the nods to Melville's *Moby Dick* are taken as Easter Eggs, rather than philosophy. \

title: Moby Dick
lead_actors: Gillian Anderson as Elizabeth Ahab (a character added for this adaptation)
awards: Nominated for several Gemini Awards (Canada), including Best Performance by an Actor in a Leading Role (William Hurt)
Praised for its high production values and strong performances, especially William Hurt's more introspective Ahab
Noted for taking creative liberties while remaining thematically faithful to Melville's novel
music: Score composed by Richard G. Mitchell
maritime_connections: Again with the *Pequod*!
reflection: William Hurt's Captain Ahab is less manic than earlier portrayals: haunted rather than possessed. Ethan Hawke brings moral conflict to Starbuck, and is the best Starbuck of the bunch, but Eddie Marsan's Stubb is too serious. This version gives us Ahab's wife, (played by Gillian Anderson) which adds a new domestic dimension, so that Ahab's obsession is not only tragic but personally ruinous to his family. Unfortunately, we don't return to Mrs. Ahab at the end of the movie, for closure. Unlike the raw tones of earlier adaptations, particularly Stewart's (who we call Picard in our house) this version emphasizes psychological nuance and moral ambiguity. This version has the best seagoing footage, being filmed off Halifax on a topsail schooner called Activ. \

title: Morituri
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Morituri%201965/awards/))
maritime_connections: WWII sabotage on a German merchant ship.

title: Mr. Roberts
director: John Ford, Mervyn LeRoy, Joshua Logan (uncredited)
lead_actors: Henry Fonda as Lieutenant (j.g.) Douglas A. "Doug" Roberts
Jack Lemmon as Ensign Frank Pulver
awards: Won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor (Jack Lemmon)
Nominated for Best Picture, Best Sound Recording, and Best Screenplay (Adapted) at the Academy Awards
maritime_connections: It takes place mostly aboard the USS *Reluctant*, a U.S. Navy cargo ship during the final months of World War II. Unlike the front-line combat films of the era, *Mr. Roberts* focuses on the routine and camaraderie among the crew members on supply runs in the Pacific Theater.
reflection: I much prefer movies filmed on real ships (e.g., *Captain Phillips* (2013)). Movies filmed in the 20 years after WWII use real ships and often some of the cast are Navy veterans, so that there is an undercurrent of realism that is missed in movies like *Greyhound* (2020). For example, Mr. Roberts' cabin door has a panel labelled "kickout panel." Or in *Action in the North Atlantic* (1943), Near the end of the picture, as the ship is nearing Murmansk, several Russian airplanes fly out to meet it. One of the pilots keeps gunning his engine in short bursts. There are three short bursts followed by a long one. Movie audiences of the 1940s would immediately recognize this as the three dots and a dash of the Morse code \"V\". \"V for Victory\" was heavily used as a slogan during World War II. The USS *Reluctant* is a real ship, the 176ft, 705 ton, USS *Hewell* (AG-145), a U.S. Navy Camano-class cargo ship. The USS *Caine* was the USS *Thompson* (DMS-38), a Gleaves-class destroyer that had been converted into a fast minesweeper (DMS). *Mr. Roberts* is a softer version of *The Caine Mutiny* (1954), released only a year earlier. In that movie Captain Queeg is odd, tyrannical, and incompetent. In *Mr. Roberts* (1955), Captain Morton (Cagney) is [odd and tyrannical](https://youtu.be/BIUn_B5s8xk?si=mjtMEsvaCWuT9bwP), but not too incompetent to run a supply ship. Lives are not at stake, as they were aboard the *Caine*. In both movies, the crew provoke and tease their captains with shenanigans, something not seen in movies like *Amistad* or *Battleship Potemkin*. Mr. Roberts* (1955) is clearly a John Ford film, with personal character and sacrifice playing a central role, as Mr. Roberts "sticks by his word," and passes by opportunities to keep his word about asking for transfers. Women are only worth looking at.

title: Murphy's War
maritime_connections: Set on the Orinoco River in Venezuela in the final days of WWII. The sole survivor of a British merchant ship sunk by a U-boat obsessively repairs a wrecked seaplane to pursue and destroy the submarine that killed his crew, refusing to accept the war is over.

title: Mutiny on the Bounty
lead_actors: Charles Laughton as Captain William Bligh
Franchot Tone as Midshipman Roger Byam
awards: Won the Academy Award for Best Picture
Nominated for 7 additional Academy Awards, including Best Actor for Laughton, Gable, and Tone---marking the only time three actors from the same film were nominated in that category
Selected for preservation in the U.S. National Film Registry for its cultural, historical, and aesthetic significance
maritime_connections: The maritime classic.
reflection: Charles Laughton's portrayal of Captain Bligh is chillingly authoritarian, a man who rules through fear, while Clark Gable's Fletcher Christian stands as a rugged, passionate symbol of resistance and dignity. It's really a classic man v. man, good vs. evil. There is not a lot of nuance compared *The Bounty* (1984). Who cares? The superb acting is truly a pleasure in and of itself. I could probably be entertained by Clark Gable reading a menu. Later adaptations work through personal backstories or romantic subplots, but this version focuses on leadership, honor, and the collective struggle of a crew pushed to the brink. It has some of the best early ocean cinematography, too.

title: Mutiny on the Bounty
lead_actors: Trevor Howard as Captain William Bligh
Richard Harris as Seaman John Mills
awards: Nominated for 7 Academy Awards, including Best Picture and Best Cinematography
maritime_connections: The *Bounty*!
reflection: Marlon Brando's portrayal of Fletcher Christian is radically different from Clark Gable's. it could be put kindly as a soft-spoken, aristocratic rebel dressed in fancy clothes with a curious accent and growing disrespect for Bligh. Or, as Pauline Kael, says, a fop. Trevor Howard's Captain Bligh is more restrained and calculating than Charles Laughton's tyrant, shifting the dynamic from the simple hero-villain to a battle of complex wills. The film expands the role of Tahiti and its people, which is probably a good thing, as there seems little doubt that a desire to return to Tahiti motivated some mutineers. But how tie it all together, the Bligh, the Christian, and the Islands? That is the problem with this movie, it almost seems like a situation where too many cooks spoiled the pot. There were good ideas in the film that failed to form a cohesive story. In the evolution of Mutiny on the Bounty films, as Bligh moved rather nicely from despot to flawed human, Brando's Christian veered off course to be corrected by Gibson, and the complex role of Tahiti and its people probably has never had the treatment it deserves. \

title: Narvik
lead_actors: Carl Martin Eggesbø as Gunnar Tofte
Henrik Mestad as General Carl Gustav Fleischer
maritime_connections: Norwegian WWII drama about the twin land and naval Battles of Narvik in 1940, where Norway inflicted the first land defeat on German forces. Follows both the military campaign and the human cost to a Norwegian family in the occupied port town.

title: North Sea
lead_actors: Bill Blewitt (real fisherman/postman cast in a dramatized documentary role)
awards: None documented (noted instead for influence on documentary filmmaking)
maritime_connections: A docudrama-style reconstruction of a 1937 incident where the Aberdeen trawler *John Gillman* was caught in a North Sea storm and rescued with the aid of coastal radio operators in Wick. The film highlights the dangers of fishing life, the use of maritime radio distress calls, and the coordination of coastguard rescue efforts.

title: North Sea Hijack
lead_actors: Roger Moore as Rufus Excalibur ffolkes
James Mason as Admiral Sir Francis Brindsen
maritime_connections: A cat-loving, misanthropic counterterrorism expert is the only man who can stop a gang that has seized a North Sea oil production platform and threatens to blow it up unless paid a massive ransom. The film was mostly shot aboard real ships and platforms. Also known as 'ffolkes.'

title: Nowhere
maritime_connections: A pregnant woman escaping a fascist European state is locked inside a shipping container that falls overboard and drifts alone in the open ocean. She gives birth alone at sea and must fight to survive on the waves with no food, water or rescue in sight.

title: O Descobrimento do Brasil
lead_actors: Álvaro Costa as Pedro Álvares Cabral
maritime_connections: Brazilian historical drama depicting the arrival of Portuguese explorer Pedro Álvares Cabral and his fleet on the coast of Brazil in 1500. Features period sailing ships and the first contact between the Portuguese and the indigenous Tupi people.

title: On the Beach
lead_actors: Gregory Peck as Commander Dwight Towers
Anthony Perkins as Lt. Peter Holmes
awards: Nominated for 2 Academy Awards
maritime_connections: Submarine crew is central to the story — a U.S. submarine is sent to investigate radiation fallout in a post‑nuclear‑war setting.
reflection: The submarine was needed. A grim meditation on nuclear war, blending maritime themes with existential dread.

title: On the Waterfront
lead_actors: Eva Marie Saint as Edie Doyle
Lee J. Cobb as Johnny Friendly
awards: Academy Award for Best Picture, 1955
Academy Award for Best Director, Elia Kazan, 1955
Academy Award for Best Actor, Marlon Brando, 1955
Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress, Eva Marie Saint, 1955
Academy Award for Best Screenplay, Budd Schulberg, 1955
Academy Award for Best Cinematography (B&W), 1955
maritime_connections: Set on the docks of Hoboken, New Jersey, the film exposes the grip of corrupt longshoremen's union bosses on the waterfront workforce. The entire drama unfolds in the maritime labour world of the New York-New Jersey harbour.

title: Operation Mekong
maritime_connections: Based on the true story of the 2011 Mekong River massacre in which 13 Chinese sailors were murdered on their cargo boats on the Mekong River between Myanmar and Laos. A Chinese law enforcement team goes undercover to bring the perpetrators to justice.

title: Operation Seawolf
lead_actors: Dolph Lundgren as Captain Hans Kessler
Frank Grillo as Lt. Cmdr. Wade McClusky
maritime_connections: Low-budget WWII action film about a German U-boat commander on a final mission to attack the American coast. Marred by unconvincing CGI submarine sequences and an interior set that looked implausibly new.

title: Passage to Marseilles
maritime_connections: Five escaped prisoners from Devil's Island are picked up by a Free French merchant vessel and join the fight to liberate France. Features prolonged ocean sequences, a lifeboat confrontation, and a German air attack on the ship.

title: Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl
PQ 17
maritime_connections: Soviet television production based on the tragic story of Convoy PQ 17, which in July 1942 was ordered to scatter by the British Admiralty, leaving the merchant ships exposed to devastating German air and submarine attacks. Twenty-four of 35 ships were sunk. Note: the watch link may be to a related documentary rather than this specific production; year and details are uncertain.

title: PT-109
lead_actors: Cliff Robertson as Lt. John F. Kennedy
Ty Hardin as Ensign Leonard Thom
James Gregory as Capt. Frederick Conklin
Robert Culp as Ensign George Ross
maritime_connections: Romanticised account of John F. Kennedy's WWII command of torpedo boat PT-109 in the South Pacific, its ramming by a Japanese destroyer, and his crew's survival and rescue. Filmed in the Florida Keys using converted USAAF crash rescue boats as stand-ins for the original Elco PT boats.

title: Reap the Wild Wind
awards: Academy Award for Best Special Effects, 1943
maritime_connections: 19th-century maritime salvage drama set among the wreckers and salvagers of the Florida Keys. Rivals compete to control the lucrative salvage trade while a giant squid lurks in the depths where wrecked ships lie. DeMille spectacle with authentic period sailing ships.

title: Romance on the High Seas
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Romance%20on%20the%20High%20Seas%201948/awards/))
music: Songs by Jule Styne/Sammy Cahn; score by [Ray Heindorf](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ray_Heindorf).
maritime_connections: Musical comedy aboard a cruise liner.

title: Run Silent, Run Deep
lead_actors: Burt Lancaster as Lieutenant Jim Bledsoe
Jack Warden as Yeoman 1st Class Mueller
awards: Nominated for a BAFTA Award for Best Film from Any Source (1959)
Recognized as one of the classic submarine war films of Hollywood's Golden Age
maritime_connections: Most of the movie is spent aboard the fictional USS *Nerka*
reflection: Captain Richardson pulls an Ahab---he violates orders to stay away from the Bungo Straits to take revenge on "Bungo Pete," the Japanese captain who sank him on an earlier patrol. The movie focuses on the dynamics between Lt. Bledsoe, the 1^st^ officer, and Richardson, and on Bledsoe eventual command. Similarly, *Das Boot* (1981) explored personalities, but was less about interpersonal conflict than individual reactions to misguided orders and the pressures of fighting a losing war. In WWII, American subs were larger and more advanced than German U-boats, but the *Nerka* seemed much less realistic than the gritty U-96. If I had to watch either sub movie today, I would probably pick *Das Boot* (1981), but there is something about Clark Gable's performance that really mesmerizes me---I think it is the absolute confidence in command that he gives Richardson, like the confidence that actors like Robert Downey Jr. give to their superhero characters. It is also interesting to compare the [depth charged *Nerka*](https://youtu.be/ruVTaJWQKHM?si=lkUHA5I_UbiCfZsE) to that of [the U-96](https://youtu.be/yD3rKts9v7w?si=_S76K_bT_koYTp0a). Fear seems to seep through the hull of the U-96.

title: Sailor of the King
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Sailor%20of%20the%20King%201953/awards/))
maritime_connections: WWII destroyer duel inspired by C.S. Forester novel.

title: Satan's Triangle
maritime_connections: A Coast Guard helicopter rescues a woman found alone on a derelict sailing yacht in the Bermuda Triangle. As the crew investigates the mysterious deaths of those on board, supernatural forces in the Triangle begin to affect the survivors.

title: Sea Devils
maritime_connections: Set during the Napoleonic Wars, a young smuggler transports a British spy across the Channel to France aboard his boat, filmed around the real Channel Islands. Supposedly based on Victor Hugo's Toilers of the Sea, but shares little with the source material beyond the location.

title: Sea Fury
maritime_connections: Transitional-era drama (silent with some sound elements) about passion and rivalry at sea. A different film from the 1958 British production of the same name.

title: Sea Fury
maritime_connections: Set in the world of ocean-going salvage and rescue tugs. A volatile new crewman joins an aging Spanish tugboat captain's vessel and challenges his authority while competing for the same woman. The tug's dangerous work in Atlantic waters is central to the story.

title: Sea Tiger
maritime_connections: Low-budget maritime drama set aboard a salvage vessel.

title: Sea Wife
maritime_connections: When a troopship is torpedoed in the Far East during WWII, four survivors — including a nun who keeps her identity secret — share a lifeboat. The film explores race, class, religious identity and doomed love in close quarters. Does not end as the viewer might expect.

title: Seagulls over Sorrento
lead_actors: Gene Kelly as Lieut. Brad Dunbar
maritime_connections: Based on a stage play. British and American naval scientists on a remote Scottish island test experimental underwater weapons. Explores the friction and camaraderie between British sailors and their American counterparts assigned to the project.

title: Secrets of the Dead: Slave Ship Mutiny
lead_actors: Featuring historians and researchers such as Professor Stephen Lubkemann
awards: Part of the acclaimed *Secrets of the Dead* documentary series
maritime_connections: This one-hour documentary is a reenactment of the enslaved Malagasy uprising aboard the Dutch East India Company vessel *Meermin* in 1776 as it voyaged from Madagascar to Cape Town, and a reflection on Cape Town's slave history.
reflection: We lump slave mutinies on slave-ships together as groups of brave and desperate people fighting for not only their freedom but their lives. That lumping is unfair, it is like lumping all the silver-star winners together as having just one war story. The *Meermin* slaves were, believe it or not, told to clean some Malagasy spears to---and clean them they did with Dutch blood! About half the sixty crew were killed. A surviving VOC (Vereenigde Oost-Indische Compagnie) official aboard betrayed the now free Malagasy and continued to South Africa rather than back to Madagascar as promised. As the ship lay a mile off the beach at Struisbaai, about 250 km Southeast of Cape Town, the trapped crew communicated with the VOC magistrate ashore via a message in a bottle that floated in with the tide. Eventually, the mutiny leaders were captured but not executed. They were sent to Robben Island. *Meermin*'s senior officers and the VOC officials were all severely punished. The captain was stripped of rank, forfeited pay, and was forced to work back to Holland as a deckhand. This documentary, regardless of the production quality, which was fine for 2010, importantly told a unique story about a slave-ship route that is less well known in the West than the Middle Passage. This film is hard to stream, but here is [the trailer](https://youtu.be/8FU9ZFkweKw?si=FJjOJ24-QXyaWziI). \

title: Seven Waves Away
maritime_connections: Survivors of a sunken luxury liner crowd into an overloaded lifeboat, forcing the officer in charge to make harrowing decisions for survival amid an approaching hurricane.
reflection: Drawing from real maritime tragedies like the William Brown sinking, *Seven Waves Away* probes ethical dilemmas at sea, paralleling *Abandon Ship* (its alternate title) with films like *A Night to Remember* (1958) that examine human nature under duress. It contributes to the genre by emphasizing moral ambiguity over heroism, influencing later survival epics where the ocean becomes a crucible for tough choices.

title: Seven Were Saved
lead_actors: Richard Denning as Capt. Allen Danton
Russell Hayden as Capt. Jim Willis
Ann Doran as Mrs. Rollin Hartley
maritime_connections: Survivors of a military plane crash in the South China Sea await rescue in a life raft while facing dehydration, storms, and internal conflicts.
reflection: This compact survival tale mirrors *Lifeboat* (1944) in its raft-bound tension, highlighting how maritime films often use confined spaces to explore human dynamics. Post-WWII, it reflects the genre's focus on resilience amid isolation, influencing later open-water survivals like *The Finest Hours* (2016) that celebrate air-sea rescue efforts.

title: Ship of Fools
awards: Academy Award for Best Cinematography (B&W), Ernest Laszlo, 1966
Academy Award for Best Art Direction (B&W), 1966
Academy Award nomination for Best Picture, 1966
maritime_connections: Based on Katherine Anne Porter's novel. In 1933 a German ocean liner sails from Veracruz to Bremerhaven carrying a microcosm of pre-war humanity — German Jews, Spanish labourers, American tourists — as Europe drifts toward catastrophe. The ship is both setting and metaphor.

title: Shout at the Devil
maritime_connections: Set in German East Africa during WWI. An Irish poacher and his English son-in-law team up with the British Navy to destroy a German battlecruiser hidden in a river. Features river launches, coastal action, and a finale involving explosives on the grounded battleship.

title: Sink the Bismarck!
lead_actors: Kenneth More as Captain Jonathan Shepard
Dana Wynter as WRNS Officer Anne Davis
awards: No major awards, but praised for historical accuracy
maritime_connections: Chronicles the real-life Royal Navy operation to destroy the German battleship *Bismarck*. Features strategy rooms and scenes aboard destroyers and carriers.
reflection: The great part is that Edward R Murrow play himself reading the news in several scenes, so the movie is worth watching for that. More did a good job acting in the scenes involving his son, but the creepyness of Captain Shepard (More) hitting on his much younger subordinate (Wynter) was hard to overcome.

title: Son of Fury: The Story of Benjamin Blake
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Son%20of%20Fury%3A%20The%20Story%20of%20Benjamin%20Blake%201942/awards/))
maritime_connections: Colonial-era adventure involving sea voyages.

title: Souls at Sea
awards: Nominated for three Academy Awards: Best Art Direction, Best Assistant Director, Best Scoring
maritime_connections: Set in 1842, the film follows sailors involved in the slave trade on the high seas, blending adventure with moral conflicts aboard ship.
reflection: Souls at Sea* anticipates the anti-slavery themes in later maritime dramas like *Amistad* (1997), using the sea as a metaphor for moral turbulence. In the context of pre-WWII films, it stands out for tackling exploitation at sea, foreshadowing how naval stories would evolve to address social injustices, much like *Captains Courageous* (1937) explored class divides on fishing vessels.

title: Sphere
studio: Warner Bros. / Baltimore Pictures / Constant c Productions
lead_actors: Dustin Hoffman as Dr. Norman Goodman
Sharon Stone as Dr. Beth Halperin
Samuel L. Jackson as Dr. Harry Adams
Peter Coyote as Captain Harold C. Barnes
maritime_connections: Based on Michael Crichton's novel. A team of scientists is sent to the bottom of the Pacific Ocean to investigate a spacecraft discovered on the seabed, and encounter a mysterious golden sphere that manifests their deepest fears. Set entirely in an undersea habitat.

title: Submarine Alert
maritime_connections: WWII drama in which an FBI agent goes undercover to root out an enemy espionage ring that is relaying information about Allied shipping movements to German submarines operating along the American coastline.

title: Submarine Raider
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Submarine%20Raider%201942/awards/))
music: Studio library score under musical direction by Morris Stoloff; stock cues typical of Columbia in the era.
maritime_connections: WWII submarine mission drama.

title: Tamango
studio: Les Films du Cyclope (France), CEI Incom (Italy)
lead_actors: Curd Jürgens as Captain John Reinker
maritime_connections: The story is set aboard the slave ship *Esperanza* in 1820, sailing from Guinea to Cuba during the transatlantic slave trade. It depicts the brutal conditions faced by enslaved Africans below deck and the tense dynamics between the crew and the captives, led by the warrior Tamango, culminating in a dramatic revolt at sea.
reflection: _Tamango_ (1958) is one of the few movies that take place aboard a slave ship. This film is worth watching--Jurgens and Dandridge became a couple after the movie. France banned the film in its West African Colonies because _Tamango_ (1958) is a French (and Italian) film about an enslaved man fighting back against his oppressor and was filmed at a time when African colonies, in particular Algeria, were revolting, or threatening to revolt against their French colonizers. In the United States, the film violated the miscegenation section of the Hayes Code (Captain Reinker, a Dutch sea captain played by Curt Jurgens, who is white and Aïché, played by Dorothy Dandridge, who is African American, kiss on screen) and was not distributed nationwide in the US until 1962. Although this is a French and Italian film, film posters used Jurgens American film name, instead of his European one, Curd Jürgens, suggesting the film was meant for the American market. The movie is loosely based on a short story by [Prosper Mérimée](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prosper_M%C3%A9rim%C3%A9e) who also wrote the novella _Carmen_. An English translation of Mérimée’s “Tamango” is here: https://merimee.byu.edu/works-2/tamango/

title: Terra Nova
maritime_connections: Portuguese drama set in the 1930s, following the men who sail from Portugal to fish the Grand Banks off Nova Scotia and Newfoundland aboard schooners and dories. Features authentic tall-ship sailing and dory fishing. Good acting and real seamanship; the plot is thin.

title: The 7th Voyage of Sinbad
lead_actors: Torin Thatcher as Sokurah the Magician
maritime_connections: Sinbad sails to the island of Colossa to find a Roc's egg and restore his miniaturised princess. The sea voyage and the mythological creatures encountered — Roc, Cyclops, skeleton — are brought to life by Ray Harryhausen's pioneering stop-motion animation.

title: The Arctic Convoy
awards: Selected for International Film Festival Rotterdam 2024
maritime_connections: Norwegian WWII drama about the merchant sailors of Convoy PQ 17 in the summer of 1942. When the Royal Navy escort is ordered to scatter, the civilian merchant ships are left to face German air and naval attacks alone across the Arctic.

title: The Battle of the River Plate
awards: BAFTA nomination for Best British Screenplay
maritime_connections: Depicts the 1939 naval battle between the German cruiser *Admiral Graf Spee* and British forces. Shot with real warships, adding realism.
reflection: An unusual war film—more about honor than violence. Captain Langsdorff is portrayed with moral complexity. For naval buffs, this film feels like a respectful reenactment.

title: The Bedford Incident
lead_actors: Richard Widmark as Captain Eric Finlander
Eric Portman as Commodore Wolfgang Schrepke
Martin Balsam as Dr. Chester Potter
Wally Cox as Seaman Merlin Queffle
Donald Sutherland (in an early role) as Hospitalman
maritime_connections: The movie takes place aboard the destroyer the USS *Bedford* stationed off the coast of Greenland.
