reflection: The film is a Cold War naval thriller, and a three-way cross between the failure of cold war brinksmanship in *Dr. Strangelove* (1964), the seasoned but driven naval captain in *Crimson Tide* (1995) and hatred of the target in *Moby Dick* (1956). As in *Crimson Tide* (1995), a few men (a journalist, the ship's doctor, and a former WWII U-boat commander) aboard the *Bedford* attempt to convince the captain to restrain is actions against a Soviet submarine cruising off Greenland. In many ways, this film is like *Crimson Tide* (1995), with a demanding but respected captain, who is obsessed with duty, and with subordinates who have a more nuanced view of the situation. What makes *The Bedford Incident* (1965) different is that for the USS *Alabama*'s Captain Ramsey, his actions are all business and duty, whereas the USS *Bedford*'s Captain Findlander seems to have a personal vendetta against the Soviet submarine. He ramps up his anger and attempts force the sub to the surface for no apparent reason. So, while Ramsey's actions are in a sense rational but zealous and misguided, Finlander's are irrational, endangering the crew, the ship, and the world, the way Ahab's hatred of the whale was irrational and endangered crew, the ship, and the financial success of the voyage. The Irrationality of brinksmanship seems to be a Cold War movie theme. This [trailer](https://youtu.be/uA-rgKBq7Ss?si=mg-vNHWSSBe_HCvw) is not advertising the same movie I watched. The promotions team at the production company has some real imagination.

title: The Black Swan
lead_actors: Laird Cregar as Captain Henry Morgan
George Sanders as Captain Billy Leech
awards: Academy Award for Best Cinematography (Color), Leon Shamroy, 1943
maritime_connections: Swashbuckling pirate adventure set in the Caribbean following Henry Morgan's appointment as Governor of Jamaica. Features tall ship battles, piracy on the high seas, and the attempt to drive buccaneers from the Caribbean.

title: The Bounty
studio: Thorn EMI Screen Entertainment / Dino De Laurentiis Company
lead_actors: Mel Gibson as Lieutenant Fletcher Christian
Anthony Hopkins as Captain William Bligh
Liam Neeson as Seaman Charles Churchill
awards: Nominated for Best Costume Design at the 1985 Academy Awards
music: Score composed by Vangelis (known for *Chariots of Fire* and *Blade Runner*)
maritime_connections: One of the classic maritime stories!
reflection: The Bounty* (1984) reexamines the mutiny with greater psychological depth and historical realism than the two previous films, which portray Bligh as a thug, almost a sadist. Anthony Hopkins gives a sympathetic performance of Bligh---a man more disciplined than despotic---and prone to outbursts ([as in this clip](https://youtu.be/KDc6dZxh0Cs?si=rQ2Vswv7CzMTcX9b)). Mel Gibson's Fletcher Christian is portrayed not as a hero (like Cable) nor a fop (like Brando) but as Bligh's former friend officer cracking under the conflicts of peer pressure (from Adams), a longing for Tahiti, and resentment against Bligh. doubt. This *Bounty* avoids clear-cut heroes and villains, instead exploring how duty, friendship, culture, weave to together into emotional strain in the isolation of long voyages. The cinematography was lush, and the on-location footage make me understand why Brando lived in Tahiti after filming there. \

title: The Burning Sea
maritime_connections: Norwegian disaster film set on a North Sea oil platform. After a catastrophic collapse of the seabed triggers a chain reaction, a submarine operator must rescue survivors — including her partner — from a burning, sinking platform.

title: The Caine Mutiny
lead_actors: Humphrey Bogart as Lieutenant Commander Philip Francis Queeg
José Ferrer as Lieutenant Barney Greenwald
Van Johnson as Lieutenant Steve Maryk
Fred MacMurray as Lieutenant Thomas Keefer
awards: Nominated for 7 Academy Awards, including Best Picture, Best Actor (Humphrey Bogart), and Best Supporting Actor (Tom Tully)
Won the Golden Globe Award for Best Actor (Tom Tully)
Inducted into the National Film Registry for its cultural, historical, and aesthetic significance
maritime_connections: A naval courtroom drama based on Herman Wouk's Pulitzer Prize-winning novel, However, much of the story takes aboard the *Caine*.
reflection: This film must be watched at least twice, but through no fault of the director. The reason is the switch from Keifer as hero to Keifer as coward is sufficiently masked by appeals to anti-mutiny common sense as to be missing in the first viewing. But even two viewings doesn't change the fact the movie has no heroes. Not even the *Caine* is the hero, as is U-96 in *Das Boot* (1981). The Caine Mutiny also suffers the same distraction as the book, which is Keith's love affair, but to a lesser extent than the book. This mutiny is a bit different from other mutinies in that the crew entraps the captain with tricks like passing around helmets at battle station. They push to Queeg to his limits as a leader, something they didn't have to do.

title: The Cockleshell Heroes
lead_actors: José Ferrer as Major H.G. Hasler
maritime_connections: Based on the true story of Operation Frankton (1942). Royal Marine commandos are trained to paddle folding kayaks ('cockles') hundreds of miles up the Gironde estuary to attach limpet mines to German ships in Bordeaux harbour.

title: The Cruel Sea
awards: Nominated for a BAFTA Award for Best British Film
Jack Hawkins won the National Board of Review Award for Best Actor
maritime_connections: Most of the film takes place during convoy protection in the Atlantic Theatre.
reflection: First, the film's cuts of real ships in heavy seas made me wonder how a sailor could survive a torpedoing in those conditions. Certainly, the famous scene of the *Compass Rose* [depth charging a U-boat hiding under floating merchant survivors](https://youtu.be/BajcgNIzMEc?si=lSLZnDjYURMaCXVN) is memorable, but the strength of this movie is its slow boil. The former NY Maritime Waterfront director, Richard Chesebrough, told me about how during is naval service in the Mediterranean the dolphins looked like torpedos and would "scare the hell out of us." That's the kind of tension in this film. It was never boring for me, and only got more interesting, especially since this was not a move about heroes, just volunteer naval officers doing their duty---at the end of the movie the captain remarks to his first officer, "you didn't finish with a medal, I tried for you."

title: The Decks Ran Red
lead_actors: James Mason as Capt. Edwin Rummill
maritime_connections: A rare mutiny‑at‑sea film focused on greed rather than tyranny.
reflection: The captain knew what was going on from the start, which was interesting. Also a rare mutiny movie that was about greed as opposed to tyranny.

title: The Deep
awards: Nominated for Best Sound (Academy Awards)
maritime_connections: Divers off Bermuda find World War II treasure and drugs, attracting smugglers. Underwater diving sequences feature heavily.
reflection: Aesthetically lush and moody. Bisset’s diving scenes made headlines. Suspenseful and sensual, though it never quite reaches *Jaws* territory.

title: The Eternal Sea
lead_actors: Sterling Hayden as Rear Adm. John Madison Hoskins
Ben Cooper as Seaman P.J. 'Zuggy' Zugbaum
Dean Jagger as Vice-Adm. Thomas L. Semple
maritime_connections: A biographical film about Admiral John Hoskins, who continues his naval career after losing a leg in WWII, focusing on aircraft carrier operations.
reflection: The Eternal Sea* honors individual perseverance in naval service, akin to *Away All Boats* (1956) in depicting carrier life. It contributes to mid-century maritime biopics that humanize military leaders, bridging WWII heroism with Cold War readiness, much like *The Frogmen* (1951) spotlighted underwater demolition teams.

title: The Flying Missile
lead_actors: Glenn Ford as Cmdr. David Hargrove
maritime_connections: Set in the post-WWII Navy, a determined submarine commander defies orders to develop and test the radar-guided Loon missile — a precursor to the Regulus — launched from his submarine, the Bluefin.

title: The Great War of Archimedes
maritime_connections: Japanese historical drama about the political and financial battles surrounding the decision to build the battleship Yamato. Based on a manga by Norifusa Mita, it dramatises the budget hearings and bureaucratic manoeuvres that led to Japan's most famous warship. A good film with a great ending.

title: The Hunt for Red October
lead_actors: Sean Connery as Captain Marko Ramius
awards: Academy Award for Best Sound Editing
maritime_connections: A Soviet nuclear sub captain defects, pursued across the Atlantic. Set entirely in submarines and naval command centers.
reflection: One of the best Cold War thrillers. Submarine tension is amplified by clever sound design and tight dialogue. Connery commands the screen (and the Red October).

title: The Icebreaker
lead_actors: Sergey Puskepalis as Chief Engineer Sevchenko
maritime_connections: Russian disaster drama based on the true story of the icebreaker Mikhail Somov, which became trapped in Antarctic pack ice in 1985 and drifted helplessly for 133 days before a rescue ship broke through. Explores the psychological toll on the crew.

title: The Key
lead_actors: Trevor Howard as Colonel Van Dam
Oscar Homolka as Captain Van Damm
maritime_connections: WWII Atlantic drama set among the skippers of ocean-going rescue tugs. A superstition develops whereby each skipper who is fated to die passes the key to his flat — and to Sophia Loren — to his successor. Love and mortality intertwine on the wartime sea.

title: The Last Push
lead_actors: Narrations by experts on the RIN Mutiny
maritime_connections: The Last Push* is a documentary that reconstructs the Royal Indian Navy Mutiny of 1946, when 20,000 sailors took control of 78 ships. The mutiny involved sailors of the Royal Indian Navy who protested against British rule, unfair treatment, and poor living conditions.
reflection: This good, concise, 30-minute film shows how complicated mutinies can be. This one was a combination of institutional racism, political change (as in *Battleship* *Potemkin*), and poor living conditions (as in part of nearly every mutiny). The film uses archival footage and narrations to describe the use of naval power to put down the mutiny, in a movement that contributed to the decolonization of India. The hard-line, knee-jerk reaction of Admiral John Henry Godfrey only fuelled the mutiny. He said, on air, to "Submit or perish." There were thousands of causalities in Bombay. Note that Godfrey may have been the model for James Bond's "M." But the sailors did not have the backing of the government and were forced to surrender. Between 25 and 26 February 1946, the mutineers surrendered with a guarantee from Indian leadership (not British) of no retribution. However, groups of ratings (non-commissioned sailors were called ratings in the British Navy) were arrested and imprisoned. The odd thing about this documentary was the lack of indignation about the racism from the narrators, who appeared to be academics. While the mutineers themselves are either extremely old or dead, their children's generation is still alive, and there must be family narratives that have been passed down but were not included in this film.

title: The Last Ship
lead_actors: Eric Dane as Capt. Tom Chandler
Rhona Mitra as Dr. Rachel Scott
Adam Baldwin as XO Mike Slattery
maritime_connections: A US Navy destroyer, the USS Nathan James, is the last functional warship after a global pandemic wipes out most of humanity. The crew must find a cure while defending themselves on the open ocean. 56 episodes across 5 seasons.

title: The Life Aquatic with Steve Zissou
The Long Ships
maritime_connections: Viking adventure epic involving the search for a legendary golden bell. Features longship voyages across the North Sea and Mediterranean. Noted for its lavish production and its two leads seemingly playing in entirely different films.

title: The Long Voyage Home
awards: Academy Award nominations: Best Picture, Best Cinematography, Best Editing, Best Screenplay, Best Special Effects, Best Score, 1941
maritime_connections: Adapted from four one-act plays by Eugene O'Neill about the lives of merchant sailors aboard a tramp steamer during the early days of WWII. A lyrical, melancholy portrait of men bound to the sea, directed by John Ford in expressionistic black and white.

title: The Meg
lead_actors: Rainn Wilson as Morris the Billionaire
maritime_connections: A rescue diver is called out of retirement when a massive prehistoric Megalodon shark, long thought extinct, is released from the depths of the Mariana Trench. Features an international deep-sea research station and extended ocean sequences. Contains knowing references to Jaws.

title: Moana
release_date: November 23, 2016 (US theatrical); premiered November 14, 2016 at AFI Fest, Los Angeles
studio: Walt Disney Animation Studios / Walt Disney Pictures
director: John Musker and Ron Clements (co-directors: Don Hall and Chris Williams)
lead_actors: Auli'i Cravalho as Moana (feature film debut)
awards: Academy Award nominations (89th): Best Animated Feature; Best Original Song ('How Far I'll Go')
Grammy Award: Best Song Written for Visual Media — Lin-Manuel Miranda for 'How Far I'll Go' (performed by Auli'i Cravalho)
Annie Awards (44th): 6 nominations, 2 wins — Outstanding Achievement, Animated Effects; Outstanding Achievement for Voice Acting in an Animated Feature
music: Original score composed by Mark Mancina
Songs written by Lin-Manuel Miranda, Opetaia Foa'i, and Mark Mancina
Lyrics in English, Samoan, and Tokelauan
Soundtrack released November 18, 2016 on Walt Disney Records; peaked at #2 on the Billboard 200
Signature song: 'How Far I'll Go' (Miranda); other notable tracks: 'You're Welcome' (Johnson), 'Shiny' (Clement)
maritime_connections: Maritime wayfinding is the film's entire thematic and narrative core. Moana is the daughter of a Polynesian island chief whose ancestors were master oceanic navigators, and the plot follows her solo open-ocean voyage to restore the heart of the goddess Te Fiti. The film draws deeply on the traditions of Polynesian celestial navigation and wayfinding, the construction and sailing of traditional double-hulled voyaging canoes (waka hourua), and the oral historical record of Pacific island migration across vast stretches of open ocean. A recurring subplot involves Moana's tribe having forgotten their maritime heritage and being forbidden from sailing beyond the reef — the restoration of that wayfinding identity is as central to the film as the quest itself.

title: Moana 2
release_date: November 27, 2024 (US theatrical); world premiere November 21, 2024 at Lanikuhonua Cultural Institute, Kapolei, Hawaii
studio: Walt Disney Animation Studios / Walt Disney Pictures
director: David Derrick Jr., Jason Hand, and Dana Ledoux Miller
awards: Golden Globe nomination (82nd): Best Animated Feature Film
Hollywood Music in Media Award nominations: Best Original Song in an Animated Film — 'Beyond' and 'Can I Get a Chee Hoo?'
Society of Composers & Lyricists Award nomination (2025): Outstanding Original Song for a Comedy or Musical Visual Media Production — 'Beyond'
music: Score composed by Mark Mancina and Opetaia Foa'i (returning from the original)
Songs written by Abigail Barlow and Emily Bear (Grammy winners), replacing Lin-Manuel Miranda who had scheduling conflicts
Opetaia Foa'i and Mark Mancina contributed additional songs
Signature tracks: 'Beyond' (Cravalho, billed as a spiritual sequel to 'How Far I'll Go'); 'Can I Get a Chee Hoo?' (Johnson, a callback to 'You're Welcome')
maritime_connections: Doubles down on the first film's wayfinding themes, now with Moana formally named Motunui's first tautai (master wayfinder) in a thousand years. The entire plot is structured as a multi-island oceanic voyage in search of the sunken island of Motufetu, which once connected all Pacific peoples. Her crew — an engineer, a farmer, and a storyteller — mirrors the actual functional composition of traditional Polynesian voyaging crews. The film grossed over $1 billion worldwide, making it the third highest-grossing film of 2024.

title: The Neptune Factor
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Neptune%20Factor%201973/awards/))
maritime_connections: Submersible rescue mission after an underwater earthquake.

title: The North Water
awards: BAFTA Award nomination for Best Mini-Series, 2022
maritime_connections: Dark 19th-century Arctic thriller following a disgraced surgeon who joins a whaling voyage to the Arctic on the Hull-based whaleship Volunteer. When murders occur aboard the ice-locked ship, he must confront a terrifying fellow crew member.

title: The Old Man and the Sea
The Pirate of the Seven Seas
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Pirate%20of%20the%20Seven%20Seas%201953/awards/))
maritime_connections: Italian swashbuckler about high-seas adventure.

title: The Pirates of Blood River
maritime_connections: A Huguenot colony in the Caribbean is terrorised by a band of pirates who arrive by river. Despite the title, the film is set on land rather than at sea, though the pirates' maritime origin is central to the plot.

title: The Poseidon Adventure
awards: Won Academy Award for Best Original Song ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Poseidon%20Adventure%201972/awards/))
music: Composed by [John Williams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Williams); includes the Oscar‑winning song “The Morning After”.
maritime_connections: Ocean liner capsizing disaster film.

title: The Poseidon Adventure
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Poseidon%20Adventure%202005/awards/))
maritime_connections: TV remake of the 1972 disaster film.

title: The Princess and the Pirate
studio: Samuel Goldwyn Company / RKO Radio Pictures
lead_actors: Bob Hope as Sylvester the Great
maritime_connections: Pirate comedy set in the Caribbean in which a cowardly entertainer and a princess on the run are captured by buccaneers. Features tall ship sequences, a pirate ship, and the usual swashbuckling mayhem played for laughs.

title: The Rebels of PT-218
maritime_connections: WWII-era torpedo boat (PT boat) film. Details are unverified — this appears to be a low-budget or foreign production.

title: The Sand Pebbles
awards: Nominated for 8 Academy Awards including Best Actor (Steve McQueen), Best Supporting Actor (Mako), and Best Picture
maritime_connections: Set in 1926 China, the film follows the crew of a U.S. Navy gunboat *San Pablo* patrolling the Yangtze River, reflecting the tensions of imperialism and cultural clashes.
reflection: Watch this movie together with *The Yangtse Incident: The Story of HMS Amethyst* (1957). If you can filter the racial biases, these two movies give some idea of what it must have been like for navies during the Chinese Civil War. For *The Sand Pebbles*, there is tension from the beginning, like Steve McQueen has a blister that won't heal. The *San Pablo* itself seems right on outside but all wrong on the inside, with “coolies” doing most of the grunt work on the ship. It makes me think there are two kinds of maritime movies: ones that at least in part try to describe the feeling and rhythm of life on the water (*Western Approaches* is that kind of movie). But *Jaws* is not that kind of movie, nor is *The Sand Pebbles*. That does not make them lesser maritime movies. This is Candice Bergen’s second movie.

title: The Sea Hornet
music: Score composed by R. Dale Butts
maritime_connections: A deep-sea diver investigates the suspicious sinking of a merchant ship off the California coast, uncovering intrigue and murder.
reflection: As a maritime mystery, *The Sea Hornet* blends noir with sea adventure, akin to *The Breaking Point* (1950) in exploring post-war coastal crimes. It represents 1950s B-movies' fusion of detective tropes with nautical settings, foreshadowing thrillers like *Cape Fear* (1962) where water amplifies suspense.

title: The Sea Raiders of Tortuga (Pirates of Tortuga)
The Sea Shall Not Have Them
lead_actors: Michael Redgrave as Air Commodore Waltby
Dirk Bogarde as Flight Sgt. Mackay
Anthony Steel as Flying Officer Treherne
Nigel Patrick as Flight Sgt. Singsby
maritime_connections: The story revolves around the survivors of a downed RAF aircraft adrift in a dinghy in the North Sea during World War II, awaiting rescue by the Air Sea Rescue service.
reflection: This film underscores the fragility of life at sea, akin to *Lifeboat* (1944) but with a British stoicism that emphasizes collective resilience over individual drama. In the broader maritime genre, it highlights the unsung heroism of rescue operations, bridging the gap between combat-focused naval tales and survival stories, influencing later works like *Dunkirk* (2017) that blend air, sea, and human endurance.

title: The Sea Wolf
lead_actors: Edward G. Robinson as Captain Wolf Larsen
Alexander Knox as Humphrey Van Weyden
music: Score composed by Erich Wolfgang Korngold
maritime_connections: Set aboard the *Ghost.*
reflection: Larsen is certainly one of the worst captains in cinema, played by one of the best actors. *The Sea Wolf* (1941) sets the bar for mutiny movies with the most despotic captain, one worse than Laughton's Bligh. The crew is not much better. They seem to know that Larsen is evil and are on board the *Ghost* simply from greed. This movie should have won an award, but it did not. Part of the reason may be because the tie-in with Larsen's brother was not clear, the completely unrealistic transfusion scene, and the unrealistic "water tank" set. As a side note, there is a review of this version of the film in the March 23, 1941, edition of the New York Times that also summarizes a few of the five (!) versions previous to this one. There are also several more recent versions too, with [Chuck Connors in 1975](https://youtu.be/87-BHle6YAg?si=JavahVzyXnQDvxbN), [Charles Bronson and Christopher Reeve in 1993](https://youtu.be/MpiawBRBtKU?si=bpvKeVivxWXcPcom) and with [Sebastian Koch, Tim Roth, and Neve Campbell in 2009](https://youtu.be/PCRqH2MNRgw?si=8oxJaCFASobWAP7F). \

awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Sea%20Wolf%201993/awards/))

awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Sea%20Wolf%201997/awards/))
maritime_connections: TV adaptation of Jack London novel.

title: The Sea Wolf
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Sea%20Wolf%202009/awards/))
maritime_connections: Miniseries adaptation of Jack London novel.

title: The Sea Wolves
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Sea%20Wolves%201980/awards/))

title: The Seas Shall Not Have Them
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Seas%20Shall%20Not%20Have%20Them%201954/awards/))
maritime_connections: WWII RAF rescue operation in the North Sea.

title: The Secret of the Purple Reef
maritime_connections: Two brothers sail to a Caribbean island on an E.G. Van de Stadt-designed 35-foot yacht (the Starwright) to investigate the disappearance of their father's schooner, and run into modern pirates. Notable as the film debut of both Richard Chamberlain and Peter Falk.

title: The Ship That Died of Shame
The Silent Enemy
lead_actors: Laurence Harvey as Lt. Cmdr. Lionel 'Buster' Crabb
Michael Craig as Leading Seaman Knowles
maritime_connections: Based on the true wartime exploits of Royal Navy frogman Lionel 'Buster' Crabb, who led underwater demolition teams in Gibraltar to locate and defuse Italian chariot (manned torpedo) attacks on the harbour.

title: The Silent Service: USS Sculpin Story
maritime_connections: Episode from the NBC TV series 'The Silent Service' (1957–1958), which dramatised real WWII US submarine patrols using declassified Navy records. This episode covers the patrol of USS Sculpin (SS-191), which was sunk in November 1943 with 12 survivors taken prisoner.

title: The Silent World
awards: Won the Palme d'Or at the Cannes Film Festival (1956) -- one of the few documentaries to do so
Won the Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature (1957)
maritime_connections: Cousteau spend his life at sea developing the aqualung and defending the underwater ocean, and this movie is mostly spent at sea aboard the *Calypso*, with its iconic underwater viewing bulb.
reflection: The [opening is striking](https://youtu.be/xr4FrELKfvk?si=2Mno4_WSGjICdgs3), with the underwater torches bubbling and sizzling and the scratchy, humming sound of shrimp underwater along with the sound of regulators as recognizable a sound as the pump action of a shotgun. I walked right into this movie, ready for hero-worship reinforcement, and was stunned by the sitting on tortoises, the dynamiting of reefs, the killing of a baby sperm whale, and the wanton killing of sharks and other marine life. The upside is to see how far Western countries have come in attitudes about protecting marine life in less than 100 years. I see a lot good-buddy-road-trip documentaries costumed in science or exploration. For example, climber Alex Honnold (known for *Free Solo*, 2018) climbing with friends while "investigating" global warming in *Arctic Ascent* (2024). *The Silent World* (1956) felt a little that way, too. Also, it is an interesting title, because underwater isn't so silent. \

title: The Snail and the Whale
maritime_connections: Animated children's short film based on Julia Donaldson and Axel Scheffler's picture book. A tiny snail hitches a ride on the tail of a humpback whale and travels the world's oceans, before the whale becomes stranded on a beach and the snail must raise the alarm.

title: The Spy in Black
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Spy%20in%20Black%201939/awards/))
maritime_connections: WWI German U-boat espionage in the Orkney Islands.
reflection: Also called U-boat 29.

title: The Voyage of Sinbad (The Golden Voyage of Sinbad)
The Wackiest Ship in the Army
lead_actors: Jack Lemmon as Lt. Rip Crandall
Ricky Nelson as Ens. Tommy Hanson
Mike Kellin as Chief Mate Jack
maritime_connections: Dramedy about a yachtsman turned WWII naval officer commanding a decrepit wooden sailing schooner refitted for military duty during WWII.
reflection: This is possibly the worst title I've seen for a decent movie. It makes the movie sound silly, when in fact Jack Lemmon gives a much more grounded performance than he did in *Mr. Roberts*, where he was silly. This movie was apparently very loosely based on a real US Navy ship, *Echo*, a scow of New Zealand registry built in 1892, that was transferred to the US Navy under Reverse Lend Lease from New Zealand. The real ship was used for cargo. In the movie, it is used to transport an Australian Coastwatcher to his station. Ricky Nelson is so-so at best, but he sings *Miss New Orleans* well.

title: The Wake of the Red Witch
lead_actors: Luther Adler as Mayrant Ruysdaal Sidneye
maritime_connections: Age-of-sail adventure set in the East Indies in which a maverick ship captain deliberately wrecks his own vessel partly for profit and partly for vengeance against a powerful trading company. A refreshingly character-dark film with no truly likeable leads.

title: The Wolf's Call
maritime_connections: French submarine thriller (Le Chant du loup) about a sonar operator with perfect acoustic memory whose error may have triggered an international incident. The film follows a French nuclear submarine's desperate mission to avert a nuclear war.

title: The Woman in Cabin 10
maritime_connections: Psychological thriller set aboard a luxury super yacht on a voyage around the Norwegian fjords. A travel journalist witnesses what appears to be a murder from her cabin, but no one will believe her and the alleged victim seems to have never existed. Based on Ruth Ware's 2016 novel.

title: The Wreck of the Hesperus
maritime_connections: Silent film adaptation of Henry Wadsworth Longfellow's 1842 poem about a sea captain who ignores a storm warning and sets sail, lashing his daughter to the mast. Ship and crew are destroyed in the blizzard. A tale of hubris and the sea's unforgiving nature.

title: The Wreck of the Hesperus
lead_actors: Edgar Buchanan as Capt. Joe Waters
maritime_connections: Sound-era adaptation of Longfellow's poem, though updated to a more modern setting. A sea captain and his crew face a destructive storm at sea, echoing the poem's themes of obstinacy and the wrath of the ocean.

title: The Wreck of the Mary Deare
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=The%20Wreck%20of%20the%20Mary%20Deare%201959/awards/))
maritime_connections: Salvage drama aboard an abandoned ship.
reflection: Read the book, just read the book. On the upside they filmed underwater with very early scuba gear, which may have been easier than filming ships in storms but probably no less dangerous. Sands’ (Heston) partner, Mike, is played by Ben Wright, who played the Austrian Nazi (Herr Zeller) in The Sound of Music. He also had a small role in The Sand Pebbles. Also, a young Richard Harris plays the bad guy, and it is fun to contrast restrained but relaxed acting with Cooper’s notoriously restrained but tense acting.

title: They Were Expendable
lead_actors: Robert Montgomery as Lt. John Brickley
Donna Reed as Lt. Sandy Davyss
maritime_connections: A dramatization of the exploits of Motor Torpedo Boat Squadron Three, a unit of PT boats defending the Philippines against Japanese invasion during the early days of World War II.
reflection: In the pantheon of maritime films, *They Were Expendable* stands as a poignant reminder of the human cost behind naval heroism, much like *The Cruel Sea* (1953) captures the relentless toll of convoy duty. Made so close to the events it portrays, the film eschews triumphant bombast for a somber reflection on duty and sacrifice, highlighting how small vessels like PT boats—often overlooked in grand naval narratives—played crucial roles in asymmetric warfare, influencing later depictions of guerrilla-style sea combat in films like *PT 109* (1963).

title: Torpedo Alley
lead_actors: Mark Stevens as Lt. Bob Bingham
Dorothy Malone as Lt. Susan Peabody
Charles Winninger as Oliver J. Peabody
maritime_connections: A Navy pilot, haunted by a crash, joins submarine service and faces challenges during training and the Korean War.
reflection: This redemption story at sea parallels *Crash Dive* (1943) in blending romance with sub duty, highlighting transition from air to underwater warfare. In maritime cinema, it underscores personal growth amid naval training, akin to *Men of the Fighting Lady* (1954) in exploring pilot psyches.

title: Torpedo Run
lead_actors: Glenn Ford as Lt. Cmdr. Barney Doyle
Ernest Borgnine as Lt. Archer 'Archie' Sloan
awards: Nominated for Academy Award for Best Visual Effects
maritime_connections: A U.S. submarine commander pursues a Japanese aircraft carrier in the Pacific during WWII, grappling with the tragic consequences of his mission.
reflection: Torpedo Run* delves into the psychological strain of submarine command, much like *Run Silent, Run Deep* (1958) explores vendettas at sea. It exemplifies 1950s naval films' focus on moral costs, influencing modern subs like *Hunter Killer* (2018) that balance action with command dilemmas.

title: Torpedo U-235
maritime_connections: WWII drama in which a secret mission is launched to deliver a canister of enriched uranium to the United States aboard a Dutch submarine. Based on the disputed real-world claim that a German submarine carrying uranium for Japan was intercepted near the end of WWII.

title: Torpedo Zone
awards: Special Prize of the Senate of Berlin, Berlin International Film Festival, 1954
OCIC Award, Berlin International Film Festival, 1954
maritime_connections: Italian anti-war film (original title: La Grande Speranza / The Great Hope) about a WWII Italian submarine commander who torpedoes Allied freighters but humanely rescues survivors, struggling to find a safe place to put them ashore. Won awards at Berlin for its humane perspective. Also known as 'Submarine Attack.'

title: Treasure Island
lead_actors: Robert Newton as Long John Silver
awards: Won Golden Globe Special Award for Best Juvenile Actor (Bobby Driscoll)
maritime_connections: Classic pirate adventure involving sea voyages, mutiny, and the search for buried treasure.
reflection: Robert Newton as Long John Silver makes the movie. His [eyeballs are remarkably versatile](https://youtu.be/pjShtsEYC4k?si=zsUMQ-M_b-cxpbia), and he can look scary but at the same time indicate that he is also being deceptive. His mutiny is the selfish kind, where, unlike the *Amistad*, or the *Potemkin*, there is not a greater good, or even survival involved. There are these spectra of mutinies, which occur for survival, fear, retribution, the greater good, and sometime greed. \

title: Treasure Planet
lead_actors: David Hyde Pierce as Dr. Delbert Doppler
Roscoe Lee Browne as Mr. Arrow
awards: Nominated for Best Animated Feature at the Academy Awards (2003)
music: Score composed by James Newton Howard
Featured song: *"I'm Still Here (Jim's Theme)"* by John Rzeznik
maritime_connections: It takes place in space, but the spaceships look like sailing ships and the plot is Stevenson's *Treasure Island.*
reflection: This film is much less scary than *Treasure Island* (1950). It does have all kinds of strange-looking aliens, but they seem gratuitous, and their morphologies for the most part do not make sense in the context of the film. In the 1950 version, Mr. Arrow is given rum by Long John during a storm and is lost at sea, with, in a sense, no one to blame. In this version, Mr. [Arrow's lifeline is cut by a spider alien](https://youtu.be/3OL3N4oqY7U?si=tCKtTIpADhO-sIG7) and the "accident" in blamed on Jim for not checking the lifelines. That is a compelling plot variation, because now we feel Jim is a victim. \

title: True Spirit
maritime_connections: Dramatisation of Australian teenager Jessica Watson's 2009–2010 solo non-stop circumnavigation of the globe at age 16. Watson sailed a 10-metre yacht across the Southern Ocean, rounding Cape Horn in some of the world's most treacherous seas.

title: Two Years Before the Mast
lead_actors: Brian Donlevy as Richard Henry Dana Jr.
Howard Da Silva as Mr. Brown
Albert Dekker as Capt. Francis A. Thompson
maritime_connections: The movie takes place almost entirely at sea, aboard the *Pilgrim*
reflection: Of course, this film is nothing like Dana's book. However, it has some interesting points. The mutiny involves the ship owner's son and indirectly a female passenger (who is not a captive). It is rare to have females directly involved in mutinies which makes it even more interesting that female-led slave ship mutinies have not been made into film. Captain Thompson was also an interesting character because he had a little backstory, as a disgraced naval officer who still wanted to be in the navy. In most mutiny movies it is simply enough for the captain to be brutal, so this was an interesting feature of the film. \

title: Two-Man Submarine
lead_actors: Tom Neal as Ensign Bob Drake
maritime_connections: WWII B-movie in which the crew of a two-man experimental submarine must outwit enemy agents who have stolen top-secret underwater technology.

title: Tyrant of the Sea
lead_actors: Rhys Williams as First Officer Davis
Lester Matthews as Adm. Lord Nelson
awards: None noted
music: Score by Mischa Bakaleinikoff (uncredited music director)
maritime_connections: A Royal Navy drama set during the Napoleonic Wars, centered on a harsh captain whose command style sparks mutiny fears. Heavy use of shipboard scenes and naval customs.
reflection: Though modest in production, it’s an early postwar depiction of authoritarian naval leadership. Its framing of discipline versus morale resonates in other maritime films like *The Caine Mutiny* (1954).

title: U-67
maritime_connections: Not really a submarine film: U-67 is merely the backstory. A disputed inheritance hinges on documents lost when a ship was sunk by the German submarine U-67 during WWI. The directors splice real U-boat footage at the opening as atmospheric prologue. Also known as 'The Sea Ghost.'

title: U-boat Prisoner
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=U-boat%20Prisoner%201944/awards/))
music: Studio library score under musical direction by Mischa Bakaleinikoff; stock cues common at Columbia in this era.
maritime_connections: WWII espionage on a German U-boat.
reflection: Also called Dangerous Mists.

title: Under Siege
lead_actors: Tommy Lee Jones as William Strannix
maritime_connections: An ex-Navy SEAL demoted to ship's cook is the only man aboard the battleship USS Missouri capable of stopping a group of mercenaries who seize the vessel and its nuclear missiles. Entirely set aboard the real decommissioned battleship.

title: USS Seaviper
maritime_connections: Low-budget WWII submarine action film. Details unverified.

title: Vanishing Voyage
lead_actors: Michael Rennie as Dr. Paul Manning
maritime_connections: This appears to be an alternate title for 'Dangerous Crossing' (1953) — a mystery set aboard an ocean liner in which a newlywed woman's husband vanishes immediately after boarding, with no record of him ever existing. See also: Dangerous Crossing (1953).

title: Voyage of the Damned
lead_actors: Oskar Werner as Professor Egon Kreisler
Max von Sydow as Captain Gustav Schröder
James Mason as Admiral Wilhelm Canaris
awards: Nominated for 3 Academy Awards: Best Supporting Actress (Lee Grant), Best Original Score, and Best Adapted Screenplay
Won a Golden Globe for Best Supporting Actress (Lee Grant)
maritime_connections: It takes place primarily aboard the *St. Louis*, and is a historical drama based on real events.
reflection: The director Stuart Rosenberg also directed *Cool Hand* Luke (1967), which is a much better film. This one tries to tell the stories of many passengers, all at once, and it gets very choppy. Based on true events, the film follows the 1939 voyage of over 900 Jewish passengers of the *St. Louis* as they desperately seek asylum, only to be turned away by multiple nations. In the movie, once the passengers realize they might be headed back to Germany, they mutiny. The passengers, overall, are relatively well treated aboard, so the need to mutiny comes from fear, as in a slave ship mutiny. Neither enslaved Africans on the Middle Passage, nor the Jews headed back to 1939 Germany, want to go so their primary motivation to mutiny is fear. Of course, the slaves were also brutalized, only adding to the desperation to mutiny. As an aside, Max von Sydow's portrayal of the compassionate Captain Schröder is powerful and contrasts with the ship's political commissar. [The trailer is excellent](https://youtu.be/rJ3aK-ClJn4?si=AIA42iiJZ7oOoBF7). In the trailer, note the phase "a human cargo," when refereeing to the Jewish passengers.

title: Voyage to the Bottom of the Sea
War Sailor
lead_actors: Pål Sverre Hagen as Sigbjørn Kvalen
maritime_connections: Three-episode Norwegian miniseries (also released as a film) about the psychological and physical trauma endured by sailors in the Norwegian merchant marine during WWII — and the long aftermath of that trauma on a sailor and his family. A somber, realistic portrait.

title: Warship
lead_actors: David Savile as Cmdr. Richard Beaumont
maritime_connections: British television drama series following the crew of a Royal Navy warship. Ran from 1973 to 1977 on the BBC.

title: Waterworld
music: Score composed by James Newton Howard
maritime_connections: Post-apocalyptic sci-fi in which the polar ice caps have melted and the entire Earth is covered by ocean. A mutant loner sails a trimaran across this endless sea, pursued by piratical 'Smokers.' All action takes place on or in the water.

title: We Dive at Dawn
lead_actors: John Mills as Lt. Freddie Taylor
Reginald Purdell as CPO Mike Corrigan
maritime_connections: British WWII submarine drama following two storylines: a submarine crew on a dangerous mission to sink a new German battleship in the Baltic, and the families they leave behind. Considered one of the most authentic British wartime submarine films.

title: Western Approaches
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=Western%20Approaches%201944/awards/))
maritime_connections: WWII convoy survival in the Atlantic.

title: What Lurks Beneath
maritime_connections: Recent marine-themed creature feature. Details unverified.

title: What Waits Below
maritime_connections: A US Army team investigating mysterious radio interference discovers a subterranean lake deep in a Central American cave system inhabited by an albino prehistoric humanoid civilization. More cave-adventure than maritime, though the underground lake is central to the climax.

title: When Eight Bells Toll
lead_actors: Jack Hawkins as Sir Anthony Skouras
maritime_connections: Based on Alistair MacLean's novel. A British government agent investigates the disappearance of gold bullion ships off the Scottish coast, uncovering a ruthless piracy operation. Set almost entirely on board various yachts and ships among the Scottish islands.

title: Wing and a Prayer
maritime_connections: WWII carrier aviation drama in which a fighter squadron plays dangerous decoy — ordered not to engage the enemy — to mislead the Japanese about US carrier positions before Midway. The film climaxes with footage of the actual Battle of Midway.

title: Yakamoz S-245
maritime_connections: Turkish submarine survival series (7 episodes) set in the same universe as the Belgian Netflix series 'Into the Night.' A marine biologist and a naval crew aboard a Turkish submarine must survive a mysterious solar event that is killing everyone exposed to sunlight on the surface.

title: Yangtze Incident: The Story of HMS Amethyst
lead_actors: Richard Todd as Lt. Commander John Kerans
Donald Houston as Leading Seaman Frank
awards: BAFTA nomination for Best British Screenplay
maritime_connections: Tells the story of the real HMS Amethyst, trapped on the Yangtze River during the Chinese Civil War.
reflection: Boring, but the real Amethyst was used. Also an important story. Used a fake Chinese person as the Chinese colonel. Didn’t explore the Chinese point of view — but why would it.

title: You’re in the Navy Now
awards: None noted ([IMDb](https://www.imdb.com/find/?q=You%E2%80%99re%20in%20the%20Navy%20Now%201951/awards/))
maritime_connections: Inexperienced officers are given command of a new experimental steam-powered ship during WWII. The condenser was experimental, not the steam.
reflection: This movie pairs with _The Wackiest Ship in the Army_ (1960). In both movies, skippers were set up with inexperienced crew on unusual ships, and made it work. This movie has an incredible cast, including young Lee Marvin and Charles Bronson, who don’t get opening credits. It’s worth watching just to see who you can recognize.
